proposals.title: Quantifying Interfacial Cooperativity: Can Supported Lipid Bilayers Mimic the Catalytic Effect of a Water-Phenol Interface on Intramolecular Bond Formation?
proposals.motivation: Classical studies show that certain organic reactions, like the Smiles rearrangement, are dramatically accelerated at water-phenol interfaces, suggesting a unique role for interfacial environments in pre-organizing reactants and stabilizing transition states. However, the fundamental physical-organic principles governing this 'interface effect' remain poorly quantified, limiting our ability to design synthetic systems that exploit it. This project aims to bridge this gap by testing if well-defined, tunable artificial membranes can recapitulate the catalytic properties of a bulk liquid-liquid interface.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the catalytic acceleration observed at a bulk water-phenol interface is primarily due to the specific solvation and pre-organization of reactants within a heterogeneous dielectric environment, which can be functionally mimicked by the interfacial region of a supported lipid bilayer (SLB) or a droplet interface bilayer (DIB).
proposals.method: We will synthesize a model reactant—an oligomer with a fluorogenic reporter—designed to undergo an intramolecular cyclization (e.g., an SNAr or Smiles-type rearrangement) whose rate is sensitive to local environment. Its reaction kinetics will be measured first at a macroscopic water-phenol interface for a baseline. Subsequently, we will immobilize the reactant near the surface of SLBs with varying lipid headgroup chemistry (e.g., phosphatidylcholine vs. phosphatidylglycerol) using biotin-streptavidin tethering or hydrophobic anchors. Reaction progress will be monitored in real-time via fluorescence spectroscopy and microscopy. Cooperativity parameters for the interfacial process will be quantified using methodologies analogous to Hunter's prior work on H-bond networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, molecular-level comparison of reaction kinetics between a classic bulk biphasic system and a engineered biomimetic interface. It will establish design rules for using artificial membranes as catalytic platforms and contribute fundamental insights into how interfacial solvation and confinement influence bond-forming events, with implications for prebiotic chemistry and synthetic biology.
proposals.novelty: While interfacial catalysis is known, applying Hunter's quantitative cooperativity framework to compare a bulk liquid-liquid interface with a precisely engineered, single-molecule-thick lipid bilayer is a novel approach. The direct test of whether a complex bulk interface effect can be distilled into a simplified membrane model is non-obvious and bridges physical organic chemistry with membrane biophysics.

proposals.title: Systematic Deconvolution of Non-Covalent Interactions in Molecular Duplex Assembly: Probing Hydrophobic and Cation-π Contributions to Binding Specificity
proposals.motivation: The design of supramolecular assemblies and functional materials relies on a precise understanding of non-covalent interactions. While hydrogen bonding and π-stacking are well-characterized, the specific, quantifiable roles of hydrophobic effects and cation-π interactions in directing molecular recognition and assembly, particularly in competitive aqueous or solid-state environments, remain ambiguous. Disentangling these contributions is critical for advancing rational design in crystal engineering, drug formulation (e.g., cocrystals), and synthetic biology.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that hydrophobic effects and cation-π interactions provide critical, quantifiable contributions to the stability and sequence-specificity of molecular duplex formation, which are not fully accounted for by models based solely on H-bonding and π-stacking.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a series of oligomeric foldamers with systematically varied side-chains (aliphatic, aromatic, cationic) to modulate potential hydrophobic and cation-π contacts. Binding affinity and cooperativity for duplex formation will be measured in solvents of varying polarity using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and NMR, building on Hunter's established quantitative frameworks. These experimental results will be integrated with molecular dynamics simulations using explicit solvent models (e.g., GROMACS) and quantum chemical calculations (DFT) to decompose interaction energies and map the spatial distribution of solvent, enabling a direct correlation between thermodynamic data and specific intermolecular forces.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a quantitative, energy-resolved map of the contributions from hydrophobic clustering and cation-π interactions to molecular recognition. It will deliver design principles for controlling assembly specificity in aqueous media and inform more accurate virtual screening protocols for cocrystal and supramolecular design by moving beyond simplistic interaction models.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated experimental-computational approach designed to isolate and quantify these often-invoked but poorly defined interactions within a well-defined duplex system, moving beyond qualitative assignment to a predictive, energy-based understanding.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Cooperativity of Phenol-Mediated Hydrogen-Bond Networks in Prebiotic Oligonucleotide Duplex Formation
proposals.motivation: The role of heterogeneous organic-aqueous interfaces in prebiotic chemistry is a key open question. Phenolic compounds, abundant from prebiotic syntheses, form structured interfaces with water that could concentrate and organize biomolecules, but their specific influence on the cooperative interactions central to early nucleic acid assembly remains unquantified. This gap limits our understanding of plausible environments for the emergence of molecular information transfer.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that phenol, particularly at water-phenol interfaces, enhances the cooperativity of hydrogen-bond networks within short oligonucleotide duplexes, lowering the entropic penalty for folding and stabilizing prebiotic duplexes under dilute, prebiotically plausible conditions.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational/experimental approach. Experimentally, we will use isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and UV-melting studies to measure the thermodynamics of short DNA/RNA duplex formation in phenol-water mixtures and at emulsions, quantifying interaction cooperativity via Hunter's established models. Computationally, we will perform molecular dynamics simulations using GROMACS with the CHARMM36 force field, analyzing H-bond network persistence and interfacial ordering. Virtual screening of phenolic compound libraries will identify structural features that maximize duplex stabilization.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, energetic framework for phenol's role in stabilizing early nucleic acid interactions, moving beyond qualitative observations. It will identify specific phenolic structures and interface properties that could have acted as prebiotic chaperones, informing models of protocell environments and the chemical evolution of informational polymers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying rigorous, quantitative physical organic chemistry tools—developed for supramolecular and pharmaceutical cocrystal studies—to the prebiotic problem of duplex stabilization at organic interfaces, focusing on the cooperativity metric rather than simple binding affinity.

proposals.title: Quantifying Interfacial Effects on ssDNA Conformational Dynamics and Cooperativity via Single-Molecule FRET
proposals.motivation: The conformation and dynamics of single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) at interfaces, such as those with membranes or in phase-separated condensates, are crucial for biological processes like replication and repair, yet poorly understood. Pre-2026, bulk studies average out heterogeneous behaviors, and computational models lack direct experimental validation at the single-molecule level under interfacial constraints. This gap limits our ability to predict and engineer nucleic acid behavior in confined or surface-bound contexts relevant to nanotechnology and drug delivery.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the proximity to a liquid-liquid or liquid-solid interface introduces a spatially anisotropic environment that significantly alters the intramolecular hydrogen-bonding network and base-stacking cooperativity within ssDNA oligomers, leading to distinct, quantifiable conformational sub-populations and dynamics not observed in bulk solution.
proposals.method: We will employ single-molecule FRET (smFRET) using a total internal reflection fluorescence (TIRF) microscope to probe Cy3/Cy5-labeled ssDNA oligomers (e.g., poly-dT or specific sequences) immobilized at a controlled interface (e.g., a supported lipid bilayer or a water-oil interface in a microfluidic chamber). Conformational dynamics will be extracted from FRET efficiency time trajectories using hidden Markov modeling (HMM) to identify discrete states and transition rates. This will be complemented by coarse-grained molecular dynamics simulations (using the oxDNA2 model) of the same sequences near a modeled interface to interpret the experimental observables in terms of specific intramolecular interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative measurement of how an interface modulates the folding landscape and interaction cooperativity within a flexible ssDNA chain. It will establish a foundational methodology for probing biomolecular conformation at interfaces and yield a dataset for validating and refining computational models of nucleic acids in non-bulk environments.
proposals.novelty: While smFRET is established for studying nucleic acid folding in solution, its application to directly probe the real-time conformation and dynamics of a single, surface-tethered ssDNA polymer at a well-defined soft interface is novel. The combined experimental/computational approach directly tests how interfacial constraints perturb the delicate balance of intramolecular interactions governing polymer conformation.

proposals.title: Direct Observation of Single-Molecule DNA Renaturation Dynamics via Interfacial Tethering and Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer
proposals.motivation: Understanding the fundamental kinetics and cooperativity of DNA hybridization is crucial for nanotechnology and molecular diagnostics, but ensemble measurements obscure rare intermediates and stochastic pathways. Pre-2026 techniques like single-molecule FRET (smFRET) have been applied in bulk solution, but uncontrolled diffusion and the inability to repeatedly interrogate the same molecule limit detailed mechanistic studies of renaturation, particularly for short, non-complementary, or modified sequences relevant to drug co-crystal design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that tethering a single DNA strand at a liquid-liquid or liquid-solid interface will enable the capture and repeated observation of its complementary partner, allowing for the direct measurement of renaturation pathways, rates, and the intramolecular cooperativity of hydrogen bond formation within the duplex that are inaccessible in bulk.
proposals.method: We will develop a microfluidic platform featuring a functionalized oil-water interface or a passivated glass surface to immobilize a Cy3-labeled single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) probe. A solution containing the complementary Cy5-labeled strand will be introduced, and individual binding events will be captured and monitored using total internal reflection fluorescence (TIRF) microscopy. Real-time smFRET trajectories will be acquired to track the folding dynamics. Data analysis will employ hidden Markov modeling (HMM) to identify distinct conformational states and transition rates, building on our group's established quantitative frameworks for analyzing cooperative interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, real-time kinetic map of DNA renaturation at the single-molecule level, quantifying the role of transient intermediates and sequence-dependent cooperativity. The developed interfacial capture technique will be a general platform for studying weak, multivalent interactions central to supramolecular assembly and pharmaceutical cocrystal formation, bridging our expertise in quantitative H-bond networks and solid-form screening.
proposals.novelty: While smFRET exists, its application to systematically study the *renaturation* of a captured pair at an interface, enabling repeated re-hybridization cycles on the same molecule, is novel. This approach moves beyond observing pre-formed duplexes to actively interrogate the stepwise bond formation process in a controlled spatial domain.

proposals.title: Quantifying Interfacial DNA Duplex Stability and Folding Cooperativity at Water-Organic Liquid Boundaries
proposals.motivation: DNA's programmed interactions are foundational to nanotechnology and biosensing, but its behavior is almost exclusively studied in bulk aqueous solution. Real-world applications often involve interfaces with organic phases, such as in drug delivery or analytical extractions. Understanding how the unique solvation and dielectric environment of a liquid-liquid interface affects the fundamental thermodynamics of DNA hybridization and folding is a critical, unexplored frontier in supramolecular chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the interfacial environment between water and immiscible organic liquids (e.g., 1-octanol, dichloromethane) will significantly alter the cooperativity and free energy of DNA duplex formation compared to bulk water, due to modified hydrogen-bonding networks, dielectric screening, and interfacial tension.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental strategy. First, virtual screening via molecular dynamics simulations using the AMBER force field will model short DNA strands at water/organic interfaces to predict structural perturbations and binding energies. Experimentally, we will synthesize fluorescently labeled, self-complementary DNA oligomers and use temperature-controlled fluorescence spectroscopy to measure melting curves directly at the interface of emulsion droplets, enabling quantitative determination of duplex stability (ΔG, Tm) and cooperativity parameters in these heterogeneous systems.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative framework for predicting DNA interaction strength in non-aqueous phase pairs, directly extending Hunter's work on H-bond cooperativity to complex interfacial environments. It will provide crucial design rules for developing DNA-based systems in multiphase catalysis, interfacial biosensors, and pharmaceutical cocrystal screening where organic phases are present.
proposals.novelty: While DNA at solid surfaces is studied, its fundamental supramolecular chemistry at dynamic liquid-liquid interfaces is virtually unknown. Applying quantitative cooperativity analysis, typically used for bulk solution or crystals, to this fluid interfacial regime is a novel conceptual and methodological advance.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity in Hydrogen-Bonded Oligomeric Cages for Selective Polar Guest Binding in Aqueous Media
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular cages in water predominantly exploit the hydrophobic effect for guest encapsulation, limiting their efficacy for binding polar, biologically relevant molecules like drugs or metabolites. A fundamental shift towards cages engineered for polar interactions (e.g., H-bonding, dipole-dipole) in aqueous environments is needed but remains challenging due to competition with solvent. This proposal addresses the critical need for new design principles for water-soluble hosts that operate via specific, directional polar forces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that oligomeric organic cages, constructed from pre-organized, H-bonding modules, can achieve strong and selective binding of polar guests in water through the formation of cooperative, intramolecular H-bond networks that effectively compete with solvation.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational/experimental strategy. First, virtual screening of candidate oligomers (e.g., foldamers, cyclic peptides) will be performed using molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with explicit water and density functional theory (DFT) calculations to quantify intramolecular H-bond strength and cooperativity, building on our prior quantitative models. Promising candidates will be synthesized via solution-phase coupling to form shape-persistent oligomeric cycles. Binding affinities and selectivities for polar guests (e.g., dicarboxylates, pharmaceuticals like adefovir) will be measured in water using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and NMR, correlating experimental data with computed interaction energies and cooperativity parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design paradigm for aqueous-phase supramolecular chemistry based on engineered polar interaction networks. It will provide quantitative, predictive rules for guest affinity and selectivity based on cooperativity, directly applicable to pharmaceutical cocrystal screening and drug delivery system design. The resulting cages will serve as prototypes for sensors or sequestration agents for polar pollutants.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly designing for cooperative polar interactions as the primary driver of encapsulation in water, moving beyond hydrophobic or metal-ligand binding, and applying quantitative cooperativity metrics—previously developed for networks in solution—to the intramolecular bonding within a defined cage architecture.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity and Catalytic Activity in an M8L12 Cubic Coordination Cage: From Guest Binding to Confined Reactions
proposals.motivation: The M8L12 cubic cage family, pioneered by Fujita and others, is renowned for its well-defined hydrophobic cavity and selective guest-binding properties, offering a compelling platform for supramolecular catalysis. However, a systematic, quantitative investigation linking its established cooperative binding networks to emergent catalytic function remains unexplored. Extending these studies from passive encapsulation to active catalysis is crucial for developing new enzyme-mimetic systems and understanding how confined environments modulate reaction pathways.
proposals.hypothesis: The M8L12 cage will exhibit enhanced catalytic rate acceleration and altered selectivity for specific organic transformations compared to bulk solution, driven by the preorganization of substrates within its cavity and the cooperative, multivalent interactions that govern guest binding.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a well-characterized M8L12 cage (e.g., Pd(II)-based) and employ a combined computational/experimental strategy. Virtual screening, using molecular docking and molecular dynamics (with force fields parameterized for metal-organic frameworks), will predict binding affinities and orientations for candidate substrates. Experimentally, we will use isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) to quantitatively measure the cooperativity of substrate binding, as in Hunter's prior work on H-bonded networks. Catalytic activity will be assessed for reactions like Diels-Alder cycloadditions or imine formations, monitored by NMR and HPLC, comparing rates and product distributions inside and outside the cage.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative link between the cooperative binding thermodynamics of a supramolecular host and its catalytic performance. It will provide a new, robust model system for studying confinement effects in catalysis and deliver concrete design principles for future catalytic cages, moving the field beyond simple binding studies.
proposals.novelty: While M8L12 cages are known for binding, their application as catalysts is underexplored; this proposal uniquely integrates Hunter's expertise in quantitative cooperativity measurement and virtual screening—previously applied to cocrystals—to explicitly engineer and analyze their catalytic function.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity in Guest Binding by Non-Cubic Coordination Cages: A Combined Virtual Screening and Experimental Study of M4L6 and M16L24 Systems
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular coordination cages, particularly M8L12 cubes, are established hosts for guest encapsulation, with applications in catalysis and drug delivery. However, the guest binding properties of other prevalent stoichiometries, such as tetrahedral M4L6 and giant M16L24 structures, remain underexplored. Understanding how cage geometry and internal cavity topology influence binding affinity and selectivity is crucial for rationally designing functional host-guest systems beyond simple cubic architectures.
proposals.hypothesis: The distinct geometries and larger or more complex internal cavities of M4L6 and M16L24 cages will exhibit unique guest binding profiles, characterized by different cooperative interactions and selectivities compared to the well-studied M8L12 cubes.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational/experimental protocol. First, virtual cocrystal screening (VCS) methods, as used for adefovir, will be adapted to model guest encapsulation. Molecular docking and molecular dynamics simulations will be performed on pre-2026 structural models of known M4L6 and M16L24 cages to predict binding affinities for a diverse guest library. Promising host-guest pairs will then be synthesized, and binding will be quantified experimentally using NMR and isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) to measure thermodynamic parameters and cooperativity, building on Hunter's established quantitative frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first systematic structure-property relationships for guest binding in non-cubic coordination cages, mapping cavity topology to binding function. It will deliver new, characterized host-guest complexes with potential for controlled release or sensing, and extend virtual screening methodologies to a new class of supramolecular hosts.
proposals.novelty: While guest binding in M8L12 cubes is well-documented, a systematic comparative study focusing on the fundamental binding differences imposed by tetrahedral and giant octahedral cage architectures is lacking. This project directly transfers quantitative cooperativity analysis and virtual screening tools from cubic cages and cocrystals to these distinct, less-explored systems.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity in Shape-Selective Guest Binding within Self-Assembled Hydrogen-Bonded Cages
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular cages offer powerful platforms for selective guest encapsulation, relevant to catalysis, sensing, and separation. However, the rational design of cages for high-fidelity shape selectivity, especially among structurally similar guests, remains a significant challenge. A quantitative, predictive understanding of how intramolecular interactions within the cage framework and cooperative host-guest binding govern selectivity is critically lacking.
proposals.hypothesis: The shape selectivity of a hydrogen-bonded supramolecular cage is not solely a function of cavity size, but is governed by a cooperative network of intramolecular interactions within the host oligomer that pre-organizes the binding site, and intermolecular host-guest interactions that are highly sensitive to guest geometry.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of bis-urea-based oligomers designed to fold into duplex structures and self-assemble into discrete cages via H-bonding. Guest binding affinity and selectivity for a series of shape-analogous guests (e.g., isomers of disubstituted benzenes) will be measured quantitatively using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and NMR titrations. These experimental binding data will be correlated with computational models: molecular dynamics simulations to assess cage flexibility and pre-organization, and density functional theory (DFT) calculations to deconstruct the interaction energies, applying Cooperativity Analysis (as developed by Hunter) to the host-guest binding network.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a direct, quantitative link between the intramolecular interaction networks in a foldamer-based cage and its intermolecular binding function. It will provide a generalizable design framework for engineering selectivity in soft, self-assembled systems, moving beyond simple size-exclusion models. The findings will advance the field of supramolecular chemistry by offering a new methodology for virtual screening of host performance for specific guest targets.
proposals.novelty: This project uniquely applies the quantitative cooperativity analysis framework, previously used for intramolecular folding and cocrystal engineering, to the complex problem of shape selectivity in self-assembled host-guest systems. It moves beyond static cavity analysis to treat the host as a dynamic, cooperative interaction network whose binding properties can be computationally predicted and experimentally tuned.

proposals.title: Quantifying Intramolecular Cooperativity in Dinuclear M2L3 Helicates: A Minimalist Model for Supramolecular Assembly
proposals.motivation: Understanding the fundamental principles governing metal-ligand assembly is crucial for designing functional supramolecular materials. While large, high-nuclearity assemblies are well-studied, the smallest possible discrete structures, like dinuclear M2L3 complexes, remain underexplored as model systems. These minimal assemblies provide a unique platform to isolate and quantify the intramolecular interactions and cooperativity between binding sites without the complexity of larger networks, directly addressing themes from Hunter's prior work on H-bond cooperativity in simpler systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that dinuclear M2L3 helicates or macrocycles, formed from carefully designed oligodentate ligands, will exhibit quantifiable positive cooperativity in metal ion binding, driven by preorganization and intramolecular interactions between ligand strands, and that this cooperativity is highly sensitive to ligand flexibility and metal ion geometry.
proposals.method: The project will combine synthesis, computational screening, and quantitative analysis. First, a virtual library of oligopyridine and related ligands will be screened using molecular mechanics (MMFF) and semi-empirical (PM6, PM7) methods to predict stable M2L3 geometries. Promising candidates will be synthesized and their assembly with Zn(II), Cu(I), or Fe(II) ions studied via NMR, UV-Vis, and mass spectrometry. Isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) will be used to measure binding constants and cooperativity parameters (α), directly applying Hunter's established methodologies for quantifying interaction networks. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction will confirm structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the M2L3 motif as a fundamental, minimalist model for quantifying cooperativity in self-assembly, providing benchmark thermodynamic data. It will bridge the gap between studies of intermolecular cocrystal formation and large metallosupramolecular architectures, offering new design rules for controlling assembly pathways and stability through intramolecular preorganization.
proposals.novelty: The systematic focus on the smallest possible (dinuclear) discrete assembly as a controlled model for cooperativity is novel, moving beyond the typical study of larger, more complex cages or polymers. It applies the precise, quantitative framework developed for H-bond networks to the underexplored realm of minimal metallo-organic helicates.

proposals.title: Electrostatic Control of Backbone Stacking in Supramolecular Polymers: From Cooperativity to Tunable Assembly
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular polymers, assembled via non-covalent interactions, offer dynamic and responsive materials, but controlling their assembly length and stability remains a key challenge. Pre-2026 work on H-bonded networks and cocrystals has quantified cooperativity in small systems, yet scaling these principles to long-range polymer assembly is underexplored. A systematic approach to tune backbone stacking via substituent effects could enable the rational design of polymers with programmable mechanical and electronic properties.
proposals.hypothesis: The electrostatic character of substituents on aromatic polymer backbones can be systematically varied to predictably modulate the strength and cooperativity of π-stacking interactions, thereby controlling supramolecular polymerization thermodynamics and the degree of polymerization.
proposals.method: We will design a series of oligo(phenylene ethynylene) or similar π-conjugated monomers with electron-donating and -withdrawing substituents of varying Hammett parameters. Cooperativity in dimer and trimer formation will be quantified in solution using Hunter's established methods (e.g., NMR/ITC binding studies and computational DFT calculations of interaction energies). These parameters will inform the synthesis of telechelic polymers end-capped with complementary H-bonding units (e.g., DAD/ADA arrays). Assembly will be characterized by viscosity, scattering (SAXS/DLS), and spectroscopy to correlate substituent effect with supramolecular degree of polymerization.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, substituent-based design rule for tuning supramolecular polymer assembly, directly linking molecular-scale electrostatic properties to macroscopic material behavior. It will extend the quantitative framework of cooperativity from small-molecule cocrystals to functional polymeric materials, providing a new strategy for creating stimuli-responsive or self-healing systems.
proposals.novelty: While substituent effects are well-known in organic chemistry, their deliberate and quantitative application to control the cooperativity and outcome of supramolecular polymerization is a novel, non-obvious extension of pre-2026 cocrystal engineering principles.

proposals.title: Beyond the Canonical Motifs: Systematic Exploration of Non-Standard Aromatic Interaction Geometries in Sterically Constrained Oligomers
proposals.motivation: Aromatic interactions are fundamental drivers of molecular recognition, folding, and crystal engineering, yet their geometric landscape is oversimplified. Pre-2026 understanding is dominated by the edge-to-face (T-shaped) and parallel-displaced (offset π-stack) motifs, largely derived from studies of freely associating systems. This potentially overlooks a rich diversity of interaction geometries that could be stabilized in conformationally constrained architectures, limiting our ability to design novel supramolecular assemblies and functional materials.
proposals.hypothesis: By imposing steric and conformational constraints through tailored oligomeric scaffolds, we hypothesize that unconventional, yet energetically competitive, aromatic interaction geometries—distinct from the canonical motifs—can be stabilized and characterized.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational/experimental strategy. First, a virtual screening protocol, building on our cocrystal screening methods, will use conformational searching (e.g., with CREST/GFN-FF) and DFT calculations (ωB97X-D/def2-SVP) to identify oligomeric architectures (e.g., foldamers, macrocycles) that force aryl groups into non-canonical arrangements. Promising candidates will be synthesized, focusing on systems with intramolecular constraints that enforce specific dihedral and offset angles. Quantitative analysis of the interactions will be achieved through NMR titration studies (to measure interaction strengths) and X-ray crystallography, supported by energy decomposition analysis (EDA) of the computed geometries to elucidate the stabilizing components.
proposals.contribution: This work will expand the fundamental lexicon of aromatic interactions, providing experimental and theoretical evidence for new, stable motifs. It will deliver design principles for accessing these geometries, thereby enriching the toolbox for supramolecular chemists and crystal engineers. The findings could lead to new strategies for controlling molecular folding and the assembly of complex oligomeric structures.
proposals.novelty: While most studies catalog geometries found in unconstrained systems, this work proactively designs constraints to *force* and *reveal* new interaction types, moving from observation to creation. It directly challenges the assumption that the canonical motifs are the only major, energetically significant arrangements.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity in Aromatic Stacking Networks for the De Novo Design of Synthetic Receptors
proposals.motivation: Aromatic stacking interactions are pivotal in molecular recognition and supramolecular assembly, yet their application in synthetic receptor design remains largely empirical. Pre-2026 work, including Hunter's on H-bond cooperativity, highlights the importance of quantifying network effects, but a parallel, predictive framework for multi-aromatic systems is lacking. Developing such rules would enable the rational creation of artificial hosts with tailored affinity and selectivity for pharmaceutical or environmental targets.
proposals.hypothesis: That the binding affinity and selectivity of an aromatic host-guest complex can be predicted and optimized by applying quantitative, cooperative models of edge-to-face and offset parallel stacking interactions, analogous to established frameworks for H-bond networks.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational/experimental strategy. Virtual screening, building on Hunter's cocrystal screening methods, will use DFT (e.g., ωB97X-D/def2-TZVP) to parameterize a coarse-grained model for aromatic stacking energetics in oligomeric hosts. This model will guide the synthesis of conformationally constrained oligoamide foldamers, designed to present specific aromatic arrays. Binding studies with electron-deficient/donor guests will use isothermal titration calorimetry and NMR to measure thermodynamics, correlating experimental ΔG with computed stacking cooperativity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a transferable, energy-based rule set for designing aromatic interaction networks in synthetic systems. It will provide the first experimentally validated, quantitative framework for aromatic cooperativity, moving beyond simple geometric rules. The resulting design principles will advance supramolecular chemistry and materials science by enabling predictable construction of functional hosts.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in translating the quantitative, network-based cooperativity concepts from H-bonding to the less-predictable domain of aromatic interactions, and in the de novo design of foldamer hosts explicitly engineered to test and apply these new stacking rules.

proposals.title: Quantifying Solvent-Dependent Cooperativity in Aromatic Interaction Networks for Supramolecular Assembly
proposals.motivation: Aromatic interactions (π-π, CH-π) are crucial drivers of molecular recognition, folding, and crystal engineering, but their energetic quantification is often context-dependent. Pre-2026 studies, including Hunter's work on H-bond cooperativity, highlight that interaction networks behave differently in aqueous versus organic media, yet a systematic, quantitative dissection of aromatic interaction cooperativity across solvent environments is lacking. This gap limits the rational design of functional supramolecular systems, such as drug cocrystals and self-assembled oligomers, where solvent choice dictates assembly pathway and stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that networks of aromatic interactions exhibit significant positive cooperativity in aqueous media due to hydrophobic and solvophobic effects, but display near-additive or weakly cooperative behavior in low-polarity organic solvents, fundamentally altering the stability and folding of designed supramolecular duplexes.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a series of conformationally restricted oligomeric scaffolds with variable numbers of interacting aromatic units (e.g., substituted phenyl/pyridyl rings). Using Hunter's established double-mutant cycle/isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) protocols, we will measure the free energy of interaction for each aromatic pair within the oligomer in water and organic solvents (e.g., chloroform, DMSO). These experimental ΔG values will be combined with computational studies using density functional theory (DFT) and explicit solvent molecular dynamics (MD) simulations (e.g., with OPLS4 force field in Desmond) to deconvolute enthalpic and entropic contributions and map interaction networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, solvent-dependent cooperativity scales for aromatic interactions, directly informing the virtual screening and design of cocrystals and supramolecular assemblies. It will establish clear design rules for predicting how solvent environment modulates the stability of foldamers and duplexes reliant on aromatic contacts.
proposals.novelty: While solvent effects on single interactions are known, applying rigorous cooperativity analysis—successful for H-bonds—to quantify aromatic interaction networks in a comparative solvent context is novel. This bridges Hunter's expertise in quantitative interaction measurement with the practical challenges of cocrystal screening in different media.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity in Mesoscale Aromatic Surface Recognition: A Combined Computational and Experimental Study of Graphene-Biomolecule Interactions
proposals.motivation: The interaction of biomolecules with extended aromatic surfaces like graphene is central to applications in biosensing, drug delivery, and nanotoxicology. However, pre-2026 understanding is largely phenomenological, lacking a quantitative, energetic framework that describes how multiple, weak non-covalent interactions across a mesoscopic surface combine to drive specific recognition and binding. This gap limits the rational design of bio-nano interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: The binding of biomolecules (e.g., peptides, nucleobase oligomers) to pristine graphene is governed by a highly cooperative network of interactions, where the formation of initial contacts dramatically enhances the probability of subsequent binding events across the extended surface, leading to binding affinities and specificities not predictable from single-point interactions.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchical approach combining molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using the OPLS-AA force field with explicit solvent and enhanced sampling techniques (e.g., umbrella sampling) to calculate binding free energy landscapes for model oligomers. These computational models, parameterized and validated against experimental data, will be used to perform virtual screening of biomolecular sequences for graphene affinity. Experimentally, we will synthesize a library of fluorescently labeled peptide and DNA oligomers and use isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and surface plasmon resonance (SPR) to measure binding thermodynamics (ΔG, ΔH, TΔS) and kinetics directly, enabling a direct comparison with computed cooperativity parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative, energy-based model for cooperativity in mesoscopic aromatic surface recognition, moving beyond qualitative adsorption studies. It will provide a predictive framework for selecting or designing biomolecules with tailored affinity for graphene, directly impacting the fields of biosensor development and nanomaterial safety assessment.
proposals.novelty: While cooperativity in small-molecule H-bonded networks is well-studied (per Hunter's prior work), applying this rigorous quantitative framework to the ill-defined, multi-modal interactions at a pristine, extended carbon surface is novel. The combined computational/experimental strategy, successful in cocrystal screening, is uniquely adapted here to a challenging bio-nano interface problem.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity and Allostery in Multi-Protein Complexes Using Extended Double-Mutant Cycle Analysis
proposals.motivation: Double-mutant cycles are a powerful tool for dissecting pairwise interactions in small biomolecules, but their application to large, multi-subunit assemblies remains limited. Understanding how cooperative networks of non-covalent bonds govern the stability and function of complexes like viral capsids, transcription factor assemblies, or large ribonucleoprotein particles is a fundamental challenge in structural biology. Pre-2026, methods to quantitatively parse intramolecular and intermolecular interaction energies in such systems are underdeveloped, hindering the design of targeted therapeutics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by strategically extending double-mutant cycle methodology to encompass multiple, spatially defined perturbations within a large assembly, we can deconvolute cooperative interaction networks and quantify the energetic coupling between distal sites, revealing allosteric pathways.
proposals.method: We will adapt the double-mutant cycle framework to analyze multi-protein complexes (e.g., a homotrimeric or heterodimeric model system). This involves selecting a network of key interfacial residues, generating a comprehensive set of single and corresponding double mutants via site-directed mutagenesis, and measuring the free energy of complex formation (ΔΔG) using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and analytical ultracentrifugation. The resulting coupling energies (ΔΔG_int) will be mapped onto high-resolution structures. Computational workflows, using molecular mechanics/Poisson-Boltzmann surface area (MM/PBSA) calculations on structures from molecular dynamics simulations, will be employed to predict coupling energies for validation and to guide mutant selection.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, generalizable experimental-computational protocol for energy decomposition in large biomolecular assemblies. It will yield quantitative, residue-level maps of cooperativity that directly inform mechanisms of allostery and complex stability. The findings will establish a new standard for benchmarking computational force fields and for the rational design of protein-protein interaction inhibitors.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic scaling of a foundational biophysical technique—the double-mutant cycle—beyond simple duplexes or dimers to dissect energetic networks in oligomeric systems with higher-order symmetry or heterogeneity, a non-trivial extension requiring careful design to avoid confounding epistatic effects.

proposals.title: Quantifying Axial Ligand Modulation of π-π Stacking in a Ternary Protein Complex via In-Cell Double-Mutant Cycle FRET
proposals.motivation: Understanding how small molecules (axial ligands) modulate the stability of higher-order protein assemblies is crucial for drug design targeting protein-protein interactions. While double-mutant cycles (DMCs) are powerful for dissecting pairwise interactions in vitro, they have not been applied in living cells to deconvolute the energetic coupling between ligand binding and specific non-covalent contacts, such as π-π stacking, within a ternary complex. A quantitative, in-cell methodology is needed to assess these networks under physiologically relevant conditions of crowding and competition.
proposals.hypothesis: The identity of an axial ligand bound to a central protein will reprogram the interaction network of a ternary complex by differentially stabilizing key π-π stacking interactions, and these coupling energies can be quantified inside living cells using a FRET-based DMC approach.
proposals.method: We will genetically encode FRET donor (mTurquoise2) and acceptor (mNeonGreen) fluorescent proteins into the terminal components of a model ternary complex known to involve π-π stacking. Systematic single and double mutations will be introduced at key aromatic residues involved in the stacking interface. Using live-cell fluorescence lifetime imaging microscopy (FLIM-FRET), we will measure the stability (ΔΔG) of the complex for each mutant in the presence of a series of defined axial ligands. The coupling energy (ΔΔG_int) between the ligand-binding site and each mutated residue will be calculated from the DMC, isolating the contribution of specific ligand-stacking interactions from the global binding energy.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a generalizable platform for energetically mapping how small molecules allosterically control protein oligomerization via specific non-covalent networks inside cells. It will provide the first direct, quantitative measurements of how cellular milieu influences the coupling between ligand binding and π-π interaction strength, offering critical data for validating and improving computational models of in-cell structural biology.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct fusion of the rigorous, energy-quantifying DMC methodology with live-cell FRET readouts, enabling residue-level energetic deconvolution of a ternary complex's interaction network as it is modulated by ligands under native cellular conditions, a significant advance over in vitro biophysical studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying Mechanical Cooperativity in π-Stacked Ternary Complexes via Single-Molecule Double-Mutant Cycle Analysis
proposals.motivation: Understanding how multiple weak, non-covalent interactions cooperate to define the mechanical integrity of supramolecular assemblies is a fundamental challenge in physical organic chemistry and materials science. While double-mutant cycles are powerful for quantifying interaction coupling in solution, their application to directly measure mechanical coupling energies under force is unexplored. This gap limits the rational design of robust, metal-free functional materials that rely on networks of hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the mechanical stability of a ternary π-stacked assembly emerges from non-additive cooperativity between specific hydrogen bonds and π-π contacts, and that this cooperativity can be quantified as a coupling free energy under load using a force-spectroscopy-adapted double-mutant cycle.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of oligomeric constructs where two interacting π-stacked modules are covalently linked by a flexible polyethylene glycol spacer, with systematic 'mutations' (e.g., H-bond donor/acceptor removal, fluorination for π-tuning) introduced into each module. Using atomic force microscopy (AFM)-based single-molecule force spectroscopy, we will obtain force-extension curves to determine the rupture force (F) and work (W) for complex dissociation. For each pair of mutations (A, B, and the double mutant AB), we will calculate the mechanical coupling energy ΔΔG_coup = (W_WT + W_AB) - (W_A + W_B), constructing a full thermodynamic cycle under mechanical load.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel experimental framework for quantifying the mechanical cooperativity of non-covalent interaction networks, moving beyond static structural analysis. It will provide the first direct, energy-resolved map of how specific H-bonds and dispersion forces are mechanically coupled in a multi-component assembly, offering fundamental design rules for supramolecular polymers and organic electronics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of the rigorous thermodynamic formalism of double-mutant cycles with the mechanical perturbation of single-molecule force spectroscopy to dissect cooperative interactions, a methodology not previously applied to synthetic, metal-free π-stacked systems.

proposals.title: Mapping Compensatory Networks in Molecular Recognition: An Adversarial Double-Mutant Cycle Approach
proposals.motivation: Understanding the functional importance of individual non-covalent interactions within complex networks remains a central challenge in molecular recognition, folding, and crystal engineering. Traditional double-mutant cycles quantify cooperativity but often assume the targeted interaction is simply 'broken,' overlooking potential compensatory interactions that may form, leading to an incomplete or biased energetic ledger. A method to systematically distinguish between essential and redundant contacts is needed for robust drug design and materials science.
proposals.hypothesis: By comparing the energetic and structural consequences of mutations designed to specifically disrupt a target interaction against a background of global stability perturbations, we can experimentally identify whether functional losses are absolute or are offset by the formation of alternative stabilizing contacts.
proposals.method: We will apply an extended double-mutant cycle framework to model H-bonded oligomers and drug cocrystals. For a target interaction (A-B), we will synthesize/engineer: 1) a 'disruptor' mutant that removes a key functional group from A, and 2) a 'global destabilizer' mutant that perturbs the overall scaffold without targeting A-B. Energetics (via ITC, solubility, or computational DFT-D) and conformation (via NMR, SCXRD) of single and double mutants will be measured. The difference between the observed disruption and the expected additive effect from the global perturbation will quantify compensation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a generalizable experimental-computational protocol to deconvolute interaction networks, generating a 'complete interaction ledger' that distinguishes linchpin bonds from evolvable, redundant ones. It will advance predictive models in supramolecular chemistry and pharmaceutical solid-form design by accounting for network resilience and compensatory mechanisms.
proposals.novelty: The approach innovates by treating mutations as 'adversarial perturbations,' explicitly testing for compensatory interactions that traditional double-mutant cycles do not capture, thereby moving from a binary (broken/intact) to a systems-level view of interaction importance.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperative Fragility: High-Resolution Mapping of Essential Interactions in Multi-Subunit Complexes via Multi-Site Disruption Cycles
proposals.motivation: Understanding the energetic architecture of large biomolecular assemblies like the ribosome or viral capsids is crucial for targeting them with drugs or engineering their stability. While double-mutant cycles are powerful for dissecting pairwise interactions, they are poorly scaled to identify which specific, buried interactions are critical linchpins versus dispensable components within a vast cooperative network. A method to systematically map interaction fragility would reveal the precise molecular determinants of stability and allostery in these complexes.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by applying cycles of multi-site mutations to systematically disrupt a single, targeted interaction in different structural contexts across a complex, we can quantify its 'fragility'—a measure of its susceptibility to disruption and its role in the cooperative network—and thereby identify linchpin interactions essential for global stability.
proposals.method: We will adapt and invert the double-mutant cycle framework to create 'multi-site disruption cycles.' For a target interaction (e.g., a specific salt bridge) within a well-characterized complex like a ribosomal subunit, we will use site-directed mutagenesis to create a series of variants where the interaction is disrupted in combination with systematic, conservative mutations at surrounding residues. The energetic cost (ΔΔG) of disrupting the target interaction will be measured via quantitative stability assays (e.g., thermal denaturation monitored by CD/fluorescence) in each mutational background. Computational analysis, using molecular dynamics simulations and network models of cooperativity pre-2026, will correlate the measured fragility scores with structural parameters (buried surface area, solvation, network centrality).
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first experimental framework for generating high-resolution, quantitative maps of interaction fragility in large complexes. It will move the field beyond cataloguing interactions to ranking their energetic importance within cooperative networks. The resulting fragility indices will offer new criteria for identifying potential allosteric sites or engineering protein complexes with tailored stability.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in inverting the classic mutant-cycle logic from measuring interaction strength to quantifying its fragility through context-dependent disruption, and scaling this approach from pairwise analysis to mapping a single interaction's role within a multi-subunit cooperative network.

proposals.title: Kinetic Pathways and Cooperativity in the Folding of Synthetic H-Bonded Duplexes
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular chemistry and crystal engineering, including cocrystal design, have historically focused on equilibrium structures and binding constants. However, the function of biological systems often depends on the kinetics and pathways of assembly. Understanding these dynamic, non-equilibrium processes in synthetic systems is crucial for developing responsive materials and controlling the outcome of self-assembly, which equilibrium thermodynamics alone cannot predict.
proposals.hypothesis: The folding pathway of a synthetic oligomeric chain into an intramolecularly H-bonded duplex is not a simple two-state process, but proceeds through distinct kinetic intermediates whose stability and lifetime dictate the overall assembly rate and final structure.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of flexible oligomers with programmed H-bonding sites, analogous to foldamers. Their folding kinetics will be measured using temperature-jump (T-jump) relaxation spectroscopy and stopped-flow techniques with IR and fluorescence detection. These experimental data will be integrated with molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using enhanced sampling methods (e.g., metadynamics) to map the free energy landscape and identify transition states and metastable intermediates. The cooperativity of intramolecular bond formation along the pathway will be quantified using a kinetic Zimm-Bragg model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a combined experimental/computational framework for analyzing the non-equilibrium self-assembly of synthetic supramolecular systems. It will provide the first quantitative kinetic models for intramolecular duplex folding, linking pathway complexity to molecular design. This moves the field beyond static equilibrium analysis towards a dynamic understanding of recognition.
proposals.novelty: While Hunter's prior work quantified equilibrium cooperativity in H-bonded networks, this proposal explicitly targets the *kinetic* cooperativity and intermediate states of folding, a largely unexplored area in synthetic systems. Applying transition state theory and pathway analysis to intramolecular duplex formation represents a significant conceptual advance.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperative Multi-Body Interactions in Supramolecular Oligomers via Isodesmic and Cooperative Folding Models
proposals.motivation: Cooperativity in hydrogen-bonded networks is crucial for molecular recognition, self-assembly, and crystal engineering, yet quantitative methods largely focus on pairwise interactions. The influence of three or more molecules acting simultaneously—multi-body cooperativity—is often inferred but rarely measured directly. This gap limits our understanding of complex systems like supramolecular polymers and pharmaceutical cocrystals, where such effects dictate stability and function.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by analyzing the stability of systematically varied oligomers using a combination of isodesmic and cooperative chain-growth models, we can deconvolute and directly quantify the energetic contribution of true multi-body cooperative interactions beyond additive pairwise effects.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a series of hydrogen-bonding monomers capable of forming defined oligomers (dimers, trimers, tetramers). Binding constants for each oligomerization step will be measured in non-competitive solvents using NMR and/or UV/vis titration, building on Hunter's established protocols. Data will be fit simultaneously to both an isodesmic model and a cooperative model incorporating a cooperativity factor (σ), with significant deviation indicating multi-body effects. Computational studies (DFT) on the oligomeric series will provide complementary interaction energies and visualize the evolving H-bond network.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust experimental and analytical framework to measure multi-body cooperativity directly, moving beyond the dimer-centric view. It will yield quantitative parameters that can predict the stability of larger supramolecular assemblies and inform the design of materials and cocrystals with tailored properties.
proposals.novelty: While cooperativity is widely discussed, this approach uniquely targets the direct experimental quantification of non-pairwise, multi-body contributions within small, defined oligomers, bridging the gap between dimer measurements and the behavior of extended networks.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity and Dynamics in Solution-Phase H-Bonded Oligomers via Advanced NMR Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: Understanding the structural dynamics and cooperativity of weak, multi-point hydrogen-bonded complexes in solution is crucial for designing functional supramolecular materials and pharmaceutical cocrystals. While Hunter's prior work has quantified cooperativity in networks and predicted solid-state cocrystals, there is a significant gap in directly measuring the kinetic stability and conformational dynamics of these transient oligomers in their native solution state, which dictates their assembly pathway.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that solution-state NMR can provide simultaneous access to structural constraints, association constants (Ka), and exchange kinetics for weak H-bonded complexes, revealing how intramolecular folding within a chain influences the thermodynamics and cooperativity of intermolecular duplex formation.
proposals.method: We will employ a suite of 1D and 2D NMR techniques (1H, DOSY, EXSY, and selective inversion recovery) on a series of custom-synthesized oligoamide foldamers capable of both intramolecular folding and intermolecular duplex formation. Chemical shift titration data will be analyzed with global fitting routines (e.g., HypNMR, BindFit) to extract Ka and cooperativity parameters. 2D-EXSY experiments at variable temperatures will be analyzed using the Bloch-McConnell equations to determine exchange rate constants for complex formation and dissociation, providing direct kinetic parameters.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust NMR-based protocol for the concurrent extraction of thermodynamic, kinetic, and structural data for weak complexes. It will provide the first direct experimental link between the kinetics of duplex exchange and the degree of cooperativity in H-bonded networks, offering deeper insight into the assembly mechanisms relevant to crystal engineering and supramolecular polymerization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the integrated NMR approach to deconvolute kinetics from cooperativity in solution, moving beyond static thermodynamic measurements or computational predictions to a dynamic, time-resolved understanding of multi-step assembly processes that precede crystallization.

proposals.title: Inverse Molecular Design via Energy Deconvolution: A Computational Strategy for Generating Oligomeric Hosts with Programmable Cooperativity
proposals.motivation: Current supramolecular design relies on forward screening of pre-enumerated candidates, which is inefficient for discovering entirely new scaffolds. The ability to specify a target interaction energy profile—such as a desired cooperativity signature for a hydrogen-bonded network—and then generate molecular structures that achieve it would represent a paradigm shift in materials and pharmaceutical cocrystal design, enabling bespoke functional properties.
proposals.hypothesis: That the quantitative models for intramolecular H-bond cooperativity and intermolecular interaction energies, developed from experimental and computational studies, can be inverted using generative machine learning to propose novel oligomeric backbones and functional group placements that match a user-defined binding affinity and cooperativity landscape.
proposals.method: We will develop a conditional generative model, likely a graph-based variational autoencoder (VAE) or a generative adversarial network (GAN), trained on a dataset of oligomer structures and their computed interaction profiles. The training data will be generated using established computational workflows (e.g., DFT calculations for intramolecular H-bond networks and MM/PBSA for intermolecular binding) on known systems. The model will be conditioned on a vector representing the target energy profile (e.g., incremental binding energies for a duplex formation). The output will be novel molecular graphs that satisfy the constraints, which will then be validated through virtual cocrystal screening protocols.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a proof-of-concept computational pipeline for the inverse design of cooperative supramolecular systems. It will provide a new tool for generating candidate hosts or ligands with predictable, multi-point interaction strengths, directly applicable to challenges in crystal engineering and drug formulation where specific dissolution or stability profiles are required.
proposals.novelty: While forward prediction of interaction energies is common, the explicit inversion of cooperativity models to generate novel molecular skeletons is unexplored. This approach moves beyond virtual screening of known chemical space into the generative design of molecules with pre-specified thermodynamic signatures.

proposals.title: Decoupling Halogen and Hydrophobic Contributions in Dual-Site Cooperativity: A Combined Isothermal Titration Calorimetry and Virtual Screening Study
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 supramolecular chemistry recognizes that halogen bonds (XB) and hydrophobic effects often act in concert, particularly in dual-site binding systems, leading to proposed cooperative models. However, the energetic contribution of each interaction type is notoriously difficult to isolate experimentally. This ambiguity limits the rational design of selective supramolecular assemblies and pharmaceutical cocrystals, where precise control over binding affinity and specificity is crucial.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the apparent cooperativity in dual-site XB/hydrophobic systems is not inherently synergistic but can be deconvoluted into additive, independent contributions, which can be predicted and tuned by systematic molecular modification.
proposals.method: We will design a series of oligomeric foldamers or duplex systems with systematically varied halogenated and alkylated aromatic residues, creating a matrix of binding sites. Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) will provide experimental ΔG, ΔH, and TΔS for binding to complementary partners. Concurrently, we will employ virtual cocrystal screening protocols, building on the researcher's established pipeline, using dispersion-corrected DFT (e.g., ωB97X-D/def2-SVP) and molecular electrostatic potential surface calculations to computationally predict XB strength and hydrophobic contact areas. The correlation between experimental ITC data and computed interaction descriptors will be used to develop a predictive, decoupled binding model.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, energy-resolved map separating halogen bonding from hydrophobic driving forces in a cooperative context. It will deliver a validated computational descriptor set for virtual screening that more accurately predicts binding in complex, multi-interaction systems, directly impacting the design of novel cocrystals and supramolecular materials.
proposals.novelty: While cooperativity in H-bond networks is well-studied by this researcher, applying a rigorous decoupling approach to the more ambiguous XB/hydrophobic interplay in designed oligomers is novel. The combined ITC/virtual screening strategy moves beyond qualitative observation to a predictive, energy-based framework.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity in Solid-State Hydrogen-Bonded Networks: A Crystallographic and Computational Study of a Bifunctional Probe
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026, the rational design of pharmaceutical cocrystals relies on understanding supramolecular synthons, but the energetic quantification of cooperative effects in extended H-bonded networks remains a significant challenge. While solution-phase studies by Hunter and others have quantified cooperativity in small oligomers, translating these principles to the solid state, where packing forces compete, is underexplored but critical for predicting and stabilizing multicomponent solid forms with tailored properties.
proposals.hypothesis: A designed bifunctional H-bond donor/acceptor probe will form predictable, extended oligomeric chains with complementary acceptors in the solid state, and the observed network geometries will allow for the experimental quantification of cooperative binding energies that correlate with computational predictions from density functional theory (DFT) calculations.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a rigid, ditopic molecular probe with known H-bonding motifs (e.g., amide/acid). This probe will be co-crystallized with a series of complementary ditopic acceptors (e.g., pyridine derivatives, amides) selected via virtual screening using the Cambridge Structural Database and Mercury software. Single-crystal X-ray diffraction will be used to solve the structures of the resulting complexes. The geometries of the H-bonded chains (bond lengths, angles, and network topology) will be extracted and used as input for DFT calculations (using Gaussian 16 with B3LYP-D3/6-311G(d,p)) to compute interaction energies and quantify cooperativity per added monomer unit within the chain.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, crystallographically-grounded experimental dataset correlating solid-state H-bonded network geometry with computed cooperative energies for a systematic series. It will establish a methodology for quantifying network stability in crystals, moving beyond qualitative synthon identification. The findings will directly inform more reliable virtual cocrystal screening protocols by incorporating energetic cooperativity metrics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in bridging Hunter's established solution-phase cooperativity quantification with the solid-state engineering of cocrystals, using crystallography not just for structure elucidation but as a direct experimental constraint for precise energy calculations of extended networks, a step beyond typical computational/experimental cocrystal screening.

proposals.title: Predicting Cooperativity in Hydrogen-Bonded Oligomers: A DFT-Based Protocol for Guiding Supramolecular Assembly
proposals.motivation: The rational design of functional supramolecular materials and pharmaceutical cocrystals relies on understanding and predicting the strength and geometry of intermolecular interactions. While computational screening is established, current methods often fail to accurately capture the cooperativity effects in multi-point hydrogen-bonded networks, which are critical for the stability and properties of oligomeric assemblies and solid forms.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a systematic Density Functional Theory (DFT) protocol, benchmarked against experimental data, can quantitatively predict the interaction energies and geometries of hydrogen-bonded oligomers, revealing how intramolecular constraints and intermolecular cooperativity govern the stability of specific supramolecular motifs.
proposals.method: We will employ DFT calculations using the ωB97X-D functional and a triple-zeta basis set (def2-TZVP) to model a series of hydrogen-bonding synthons, from dimers to tetramers, derived from known cocrystal formers. Geometry optimizations and frequency calculations will be performed to obtain interaction energies corrected for basis set superposition error (BSSE). The protocol will be validated against the researcher's existing experimental data on cooperativity and cocrystal structures, with specific focus on how chain folding and duplex formation are influenced by subtle electronic effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated computational workflow for predicting the stability of cooperative H-bonded networks, moving beyond pairwise interactions. It will provide fundamental insights into the relationship between molecular structure and supramolecular assembly, directly aiding the virtual screening of new cocrystals with tailored physicochemical properties.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in explicitly targeting the prediction of cooperativity—a non-additive, emergent property—within oligomeric H-bonded systems using a benchmarked DFT approach, rather than focusing solely on dimeric interaction energies or statistical co-occurrence in crystal structures.

proposals.title: Quantifying Donor Strength via Cooperative Binding to a Bidentate Acceptor Probe: An Inverse Design Approach
proposals.motivation: The rational design of supramolecular assemblies and pharmaceutical cocrystals relies on understanding the strength and cooperativity of hydrogen bond donors. Current methods often use donor-centric probes to characterize acceptors. A systematic, quantitative framework for ranking donor strength using a well-defined, dual-acceptor molecular scaffold is lacking. This inverse design problem is critical for predicting and engineering complex multi-component solids and foldamers.
proposals.hypothesis: A rigid, shape-persistent oligomer with two precisely oriented hydrogen bond acceptor sites will act as a universal probe, where the measured free energy of binding to a series of donors directly quantifies donor strength and reveals cooperative effects in ternary complexes.
proposals.method: We will design and synthesize a bidentate acceptor probe based on a rigid oligoamide scaffold, incorporating two pyridine N-oxide or phosphine oxide units at a fixed geometry. Binding studies will be conducted in a low-polarity solvent (e.g., CDCl3) using 1H NMR titration to determine association constants (Ka) for a series of donor molecules (amides, phenols, carboxylic acids). These experimental results will be compared with virtual screening using density functional theory (DFT) calculations (e.g., B3LYP-D3/6-311+G(d,p)) to predict binding energies and probe geometry. Cooperativity factors (α) for 2:1 donor:probe complexes will be calculated using the Hunter-Sanders model.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new, quantitative scale for hydrogen bond donor strength based on binding to a standardized bidentate acceptor. It will provide a direct experimental and computational methodology for the 'inverse' screening of donors, complementing existing acceptor scales. The results will directly inform the design of cocrystals and foldamers by predicting optimal donor partners for multi-acceptor sites.
proposals.novelty: This inverts the classic molecular recognition paradigm by using a designed acceptor oligomer as the invariant probe, rather than a donor. It explicitly links the quantitative measurement of cooperativity in small molecule binding—a core theme of Hunter's work—to the practical problem of donor ranking for materials design.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity in Supramolecular Duplex Formation: A Systematic Study of Binding Affinities for Complementary H-Bond Acceptor Arrays
proposals.motivation: The rational design of supramolecular assemblies and functional materials relies on precise control over molecular recognition. While Hunter's group has quantified cooperativity in small H-bonded networks, a systematic, quantitative understanding of how binding affinity scales with the number and arrangement of complementary acceptor sites in larger, programmable oligomers is lacking. This gap limits the predictive design of complex, multi-component supramolecular systems, such as synthetic duplexes, which are pre-2026 targets for nanotechnology and crystal engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the free energy of binding for a multi-site H-bond donor probe to complementary acceptor arrays will exhibit a strong, non-linear (cooperative) dependence on the number of acceptor sites, and that this cooperativity will be significantly modulated by the intramolecular preorganization of the acceptor strands within the duplex.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of oligomeric strands with systematically varied sequences of H-bond acceptor sites (e.g., pyridine, phosphine oxide). Binding affinities (Ka) for these strands with a complementary, well-characterized donor probe (e.g., a known oligoamide) will be measured in chloroform using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and NMR titration. The experimental data will be combined with computational analysis: molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using AMBER or GROMACS will assess acceptor strand flexibility, while density functional theory (DFT) calculations (e.g., B3LYP-D3/6-311G**) will provide interaction energies for model fragments. A modified Zimm-Bragg model, adapted for supramolecular duplex formation, will be used to quantify intrinsic and cooperative binding parameters from the affinity data.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive, quantitative dataset linking acceptor array structure to binding affinity in cooperative duplex systems. It will deliver a predictive, parameterized model for supramolecular affinity, directly informing the design of higher-order assemblies and cocrystals. The results will bridge the gap between studies of small-molecule cooperativity and the engineering of programmable, chain-like supramolecular polymers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying the quantitative, physical-organic framework developed for small networks to the complex problem of programmable duplex formation, explicitly decoupling the effects of valency from intramolecular preorganization—a critical but often conflated factor in pre-2026 supramolecular design.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity and Chain-Length in Supramolecular Oligomerization via Integrated ITC and DOSY NMR Analysis
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular polymers and oligomers, formed through reversible non-covalent interactions, are central to functional materials and molecular recognition. However, pre-2026 methods for characterizing their assembly often treat them as simple 1:1 complexes or static structures, inadequately capturing the dynamic, multi-step nature of oligomerization and the critical role of intramolecular interactions in stabilizing specific chain lengths. This gap limits the rational design of supramolecular systems with predictable properties.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying an integrated suite of solution-phase techniques—specifically Isothermal Titration Calorimetry (ITC) for global thermodynamics and Diffusion-Ordered Spectroscopy (DOSY) NMR for hydrodynamic size—will allow the deconvolution of stepwise binding constants and the quantification of cooperativity in the formation of discrete supramolecular oligomers, moving beyond simple dimerization models.
proposals.method: We will synthesize or select model ditopic monomers with well-defined hydrogen-bonding motifs (e.g., ureidopyrimidinone, diamidopyridine). ITC titrations will be performed to obtain the overall enthalpy (ΔH) and binding constant (K). These global ITC data will be fitted to stepwise association models using non-linear regression algorithms to extract ΔH_i and K_i for each addition step. Concurrently, DOSY NMR experiments will be conducted at varying concentrations to monitor the increase in hydrodynamic radius, providing direct evidence for oligomer chain length and validating the association models derived from ITC. Molecular dynamics simulations (using pre-2026 force fields like GAFF) will support the interpretation of favored oligomeric states.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a robust, generalizable protocol for quantifying the thermodynamics of supramolecular oligomerization, directly linking thermodynamic parameters to specific aggregate sizes. It will provide a foundational dataset on how intramolecular interactions (e.g., folding, strain) influence cooperativity profiles, advancing predictive models for supramolecular polymer design.
proposals.novelty: While ITC and DOSY are established separately, their combined, quantitative application to deconvolute stepwise binding in synthetic supramolecular oligomers—explicitly testing models of positive or negative cooperativity in chain growth—is novel and moves beyond the binary complex analysis common in cocrystal screening.

proposals.title: Quantitative Prediction of Cooperativity in Hydrogen-Bonded Oligomers Using Alchemical Free Energy Calculations
proposals.motivation: The rational design of supramolecular assemblies and pharmaceutical cocrystals relies on understanding the energetics of intermolecular interactions. While Hunter's group has pioneered quantitative measurements of cooperativity in H-bonded networks, accurately predicting the binding free energies and non-additive effects for complex, multi-site oligomers remains a significant computational challenge. A robust predictive model would accelerate the discovery of new functional materials and solid forms by prioritizing synthetic targets.
proposals.hypothesis: Alchemical free energy perturbation (FEP) calculations, calibrated against experimental thermodynamic data, can accurately predict the absolute and relative binding affinities of hydrogen-bonded oligomers, capturing the emergent cooperativity that arises from intramolecular interactions within the chain.
proposals.method: We will employ a hierarchical computational protocol. Initial QM calculations (DFT, ωB97X-D/6-311G**) will parameterize force fields for target H-bonding motifs. Explicit-solvent molecular dynamics simulations using AMBER or OpenMM will then be performed on probe-acceptor complexes. Alchemical FEP (or Thermodynamic Integration) calculations using the YANK or SOMD frameworks will compute absolute binding free energies.
