The approach will be validated against Hunter's existing experimental data on duplex and chain-forming systems before being applied to predict affinities for new oligomer designs.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated computational pipeline for the *ab initio* prediction of binding free energies in cooperative H-bonded systems, moving beyond qualitative scoring. It will provide fundamental insights into how intramolecular constraints modulate intermolecular affinity, establishing a direct link between molecular simulation and experimental measurements of interaction strength.
proposals.novelty: While FEP is used in drug discovery for protein-ligand binding, its rigorous application to predict cooperativity in small-molecule, multi-component H-bonded oligomers is novel. This integrates Hunter's experimental expertise in quantifying interactions with state-of-the-art alchemical methods to create a predictive design tool for supramolecular chemistry.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity in Synthetic Polymer Networks with Integrated H-Bonding Arrays
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 supramolecular chemistry has established that small-molecule oligomers with multiple hydrogen-bonding sites exhibit significant cooperativity, amplifying binding affinity. However, translating these principles to extended polymeric networks, where multiple cooperative arrays are covalently linked, remains unexplored. This limits our ability to engineer synthetic materials that mimic the sophisticated, allosteric responsiveness of biological macromolecules.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating a well-defined, cooperative H-bonding recognition motif (e.g., a three-point DDAA array) into every monomer of a linear polymer will create a supramolecular network where intramolecular folding and intermolecular cross-linking are governed by amplified, multivalent cooperativity, leading to tunable chain dynamics and bulk material properties.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of acrylate or styrene monomers functionalized with the DDAA H-bonding array, followed by controlled radical polymerization (ATRP) to obtain well-defined polymers. Cooperativity will be quantified in solution using established Hunter group methodologies: variable-temperature NMR and isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) to measure thermodynamic parameters (ΔG, ΔH, TΔS) for binding to complementary small-molecule partners. The folding and cross-linking behavior will be probed via diffusion-ordered NMR spectroscopy (DOSY) and viscometry. Complementary molecular dynamics simulations (using GAFF2 force field with explicit polarizability corrections for H-bonds) will model chain conformation and network formation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish the first quantitative framework for cooperativity in synthetic polymeric networks, moving beyond small oligomers. It will provide design rules for creating stimuli-responsive materials where properties like viscosity, self-healing, and solute permeability are controlled by programmable, cooperative non-covalent interactions.
proposals.novelty: While cooperativity in discrete H-bonded duplexes is known, its systematic integration and measurement within a repeating polymer scaffold, creating a 'cooperative polymer', is a novel supramolecular architecture. This bridges the gap between molecular recognition and polymer science.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity in Self-Assembling Capsules via Probe Dimer Analysis
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular capsules, formed through non-covalent interactions, are crucial for applications in catalysis, sensing, and drug delivery. However, predicting and controlling their assembly remains challenging due to the complex interplay of multiple weak interactions. A quantitative, generalizable method to deconvolute the contributions of individual binding events to overall capsule stability is needed to enable rational design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the stability of a self-assembling capsule can be predicted by analyzing the interaction between its constituent halves as 'probe dimers', and that this dimeric interaction energy, measured experimentally and computationally, directly correlates with the cooperativity and yield of the full assembly.
proposals.method: The approach combines virtual screening and experimental validation. First, a computational library of functionalized hydrogen-bonding motifs (e.g., based on ureas, carboxylic acids) will be screened using DFT (e.g., Gaussian 16) to identify complementary pairs with strong, directional dimerization energy. Promising 'probe dimers' will be synthesized and their association constants (K_dim) measured in solution via NMR titration in low-polarity solvents. These dimers will then be integrated into larger, shape-persistent scaffolds (e.g., cavitands or curved oligomers) to form capsules, whose stability will be characterized and correlated with the foundational dimer K_dim and computed interaction maps.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a transferable protocol for the *de novo* design of self-assembling systems by isolating and optimizing the critical dimeric interaction. It will provide a quantitative dataset linking simple dimer properties to complex assembly outcomes, directly advancing the field of predictive supramolecular chemistry. The resulting design rules could accelerate the discovery of new functional materials and cocrystals.
proposals.novelty: While Hunter's prior work quantified cooperativity in networks, this proposal uniquely applies the 'probe dimer' concept as a predictive, modular building block for capsule design, moving beyond retrospective analysis to a forward-synthetic strategy. It integrates virtual cocrystal screening methods, typically used for API solid forms, into the solution-phase design of discrete supramolecular assemblies.

proposals.title: Cooperative Templating for the Synthesis of Higher-Order [n]Catenanes (n≥3) via Programmed Oligomer Folding
proposals.motivation: While [2]catenanes are well-established as molecular machines and functional materials, higher-order interlocked architectures ([3]-catenanes and beyond) remain synthetically challenging and rare. Access to these complex topologies would enable the study of amplified molecular motion, novel polymeric materials with topologically defined properties, and more intricate molecular devices. Pre-2026, most syntheses rely on stepwise or statistical methods with low yields, highlighting a need for a rational, templated strategy.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing oligomeric precursors with multiple, programmed hydrogen-bonding recognition sites, we can exploit intramolecular folding and cooperative non-covalent interactions to template the efficient, one-pot formation of [3]- and [4]catenanes in a controlled manner.
proposals.method: The approach combines computational screening and iterative synthesis. First, virtual cocrystal screening (VCS) methods, as used for adefovir, will be employed to design and optimize oligomeric building blocks containing multiple 2,6-diamidopyridine (DAP) or related motifs, predicting their intramolecular folding via H-bonded duplex formation. Synthesis will follow, creating bis- and tris-macrocyclic precursors. The key templating step will involve the addition of complementary ditopic guests (e.g., naphthalenediimide derivatives) to induce cooperative folding and ring-closing metathesis, mechanically interlocking the pre-organized rings. Yields and structures will be analyzed by NMR, mass spectrometry, and X-ray crystallography of derived cocrystals.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general, cooperative templating methodology for higher-order catenane synthesis, moving beyond binary systems. It will provide the first quantitative measurement of cooperativity in the formation of multi-component mechanical bonds and deliver novel, well-characterized [3]- and [4]catenanes as testbeds for advanced functional studies.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in applying principles of quantitative cooperativity from H-bonded networks and computational cocrystal design to the problem of multi-ring mechanical bond formation, shifting from serendipitous assembly to a predictable, oligomer folding-based strategy.

proposals.title: Design and Cooperativity Analysis of Functionalized Catenanes: Integrating Redox-Active and Photochromic Sites for Programmable Molecular Motion
proposals.motivation: Catenanes are archetypal architectures for molecular machines, but their functionality is often limited by a lack of integrated, addressable units. Pre-2026, the field seeks to move beyond structural complexity to create machines with externally controllable, multi-state outputs. Incorporating specific functional groups directly into the macrocyclic framework is a critical step toward achieving such programmable, stimuli-responsive systems with applications in molecular electronics and nanoscale actuators.
proposals.hypothesis: That strategically placing redox-active (e.g., viologen) or photochromic (e.g., azobenzene) units within the catenane macrocycles will create a cooperative, intramolecular network of interactions, leading to large-amplitude, predictable mechanical motion in response to electrochemical or photonic stimuli.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational/experimental strategy. First, virtual screening of functionalized catenane precursors using DFT (e.g., Gaussian 16) and molecular dynamics (GROMACS) will model interaction networks and predict folding preferences. Promising designs will be synthesized via template-directed methods, leveraging metal-ion or donor-acceptor interactions for efficient ring-closing. Cooperativity in the resulting intramolecular H-bond and π-stacking networks will be quantified using established isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) protocols, correlating interaction strength with the efficiency of stimulus-induced pirouetting or translation.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a general design framework for embedding function into interlocked molecules, moving them from static curiosities to dynamic components. It will provide quantitative, experimentally validated principles for how intramolecular interaction networks within catenanes can be tuned to amplify and direct mechanical output, a key requirement for device integration.
proposals.novelty: While functionalized catenanes exist, a systematic, cooperativity-focused investigation linking specific incorporated sites to the magnitude and fidelity of mechanical response is lacking. This approach directly applies Hunter's expertise in quantifying weak interactions to the core problem of engineering efficiency in molecular machines.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity in the Assembly Dynamics of Hydrogen-Bonded Oligomeric Rotaxanes via Supramolecular NMR Exchange Spectroscopy
proposals.motivation: The rational design of functional supramolecular assemblies, such as those for drug delivery or molecular machines, requires precise knowledge of their assembly pathways and energetics. Pre-2026 work, including Hunter's on H-bonded network cooperativity, often provides static structural snapshots or thermodynamic averages, leaving a critical gap in understanding the dynamic processes—like threading and template binding—that govern assembly kinetics and fidelity.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the threading rate and template binding constant in the assembly of oligomeric [n]rotaxanes are governed by positive intramolecular cooperativity between adjacent recognition sites, which can be quantitatively measured via dynamic NMR parameters.
proposals.method: We will employ a combination of 1D and 2D supramolecular NMR exchange spectroscopy (EXSY) at variable temperature to study the threading dynamics of a ditopic axle through a macrocycle with complementary H-bonding sites. Specifically, we will synthesize a series of oligomeric model systems and use 2D EXSY (NOESY-type) experiments to measure exchange rates between free and bound states. Binding constants and threading rates will be extracted by globally fitting the exchange matrix data and chemical shift titrations using established iterative fitting algorithms, correlating experimental results with DFT-calculated transition states for the threading process.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, quantitative measurement of threading kinetics and template binding energetics for cooperatively assembling oligorotaxanes. It will establish a general NMR-based methodology for dissecting the dynamic contributions to supramolecular cooperativity, moving beyond equilibrium constants to kinetic parameters crucial for designing responsive systems.
proposals.novelty: While EXSY is established for studying molecular dynamics, its systematic application to deconvolute the individual kinetic steps (threading vs. binding) within a multi-step, cooperative supramolecular assembly process is novel and non-trivial, offering a dynamic complement to static crystallographic or thermodynamic studies.

proposals.title: Quantifying the Role of Solvent and Ion Atmosphere in DNA Duplex Stability: A Combined Molecular Dynamics and Experimental Cooperativity Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current computational models for predicting DNA conformation and duplex stability often treat the environment implicitly, neglecting explicit, cooperative interactions with water molecules and counterions. This is a critical gap, as pre-2026 studies, including Hunter's work on H-bonded networks, show that solvent and ions are not passive spectators but actively participate in and modulate intramolecular interactions and folding. Accurate models are essential for rational design in nanotechnology and for understanding biological function.
proposals.hypothesis: Explicit inclusion of solvent and a physiological ion atmosphere in molecular dynamics simulations will reveal specific, cooperative networks of interactions that significantly stabilize non-canonical DNA conformations and alter duplex thermodynamics in ways not captured by implicit solvent models.
proposals.method: We will employ all-atom molecular dynamics (MD) simulations using the AMBER force field with explicit TIP3P water and a range of Na+/Mg2+ concentrations. Enhanced sampling techniques (e.g., replica exchange MD) will be used to explore conformational landscapes. Key interaction energies and network cooperativity will be quantified using QM/MM calculations on snapshots. These computational predictions will be validated via experimental measurements of duplex stability (UV melting) and cooperativity (using Hunter's established methodologies) on designed sequences predicted to be sensitive to ion effects.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated, high-resolution computational framework for DNA modeling that accounts for explicit solvent and ion effects. It will provide quantitative, mechanistic insights into how the environment dictates folding pathways and stability, directly impacting fields like DNA nanotechnology and the understanding of gene regulation. The combined methodology will set a new standard for accuracy in predicting nucleic acid behavior.
proposals.novelty: While MD with explicit solvent exists, the novelty lies in systematically linking the simulated dynamics of the solvent/ion atmosphere to quantitative, experimental measures of interaction cooperativity within the DNA, creating a closed feedback loop to refine models and uncover design principles for environmental sensitivity.

proposals.title: Fragment-Based Virtual Screening for Cocrystal Engineering: A Computational Protocol for Identifying High-Affinity Molecular Adducts
proposals.motivation: Current virtual cocrystal screening (VCS) methods primarily focus on screening large libraries of known coformers, which is computationally expensive and may overlook novel, high-affinity interactions. A more fundamental approach, inspired by fragment-based drug discovery (FBDD), would be to screen interactions between an active pharmaceutical ingredient (API) and small, diverse molecular fragments. This could identify key binding motifs and predict novel coformer scaffolds with superior complementarity, accelerating the design of cocrystals with tailored physicochemical properties.
proposals.hypothesis: That a fragment-based virtual screening protocol, using quantum mechanical (QM) calculations of interaction energies, can identify high-affinity binding motifs for a target API and successfully predict the formation and stability of novel cocrystals with coformers derived from those fragments.
proposals.method: We will adapt our established VCS pipeline, used for adefovir and bosentan, to a fragment-based approach. First, a target API will be fragmented into its key functional groups, and a diverse library of small molecular fragments (e.g., carboxylic acids, amides, heterocycles) will be generated. Interaction energies for API-fragment dimer pairs will be calculated using high-level DFT methods (e.g., ωB97X-D/6-311G(d,p)) with counterpoise correction, quantifying hydrogen bond and van der Waals contributions. The most favorable fragment motifs will then be used to guide the selection and assembly of full coformer molecules for full crystal structure prediction (CSP) using force-field-based packing algorithms (e.g., in CrystalPredictor/CrystalOptimizer).
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a novel, rational fragment-based design strategy for cocrystal engineering, moving beyond library screening. It will provide fundamental insights into the key intermolecular interactions governing cocrystal stability for a given API. The resulting computational protocol will be a valuable tool for the *de novo* design of coformers, potentially leading to cocrystals with unprecedented performance.
proposals.novelty: While fragment-based screening is standard in drug discovery, its application to cocrystal engineering is novel. This approach shifts the focus from screening existing coformers to designing them based on first-principles interaction energetics, offering a more rational and potentially more productive design pathway.

proposals.title: Solvent-Directed Self-Assembly in Ionic Liquids: Quantifying the Role of Pre-Organized Solvent Networks in Guiding Hydrogen-Bonded Duplex Formation
proposals.motivation: The rational design of supramolecular assemblies and pharmaceutical cocrystals relies on understanding how solvents influence intermolecular bond formation. Traditional models emphasize solvent polarity and hydrogen-bond competition, but the role of structured, low-polarity solvents like ionic liquids (ILs) remains underexplored. Pre-2026 work suggests ILs possess inherent, directional solvent networks that could pre-organize solutes, offering a novel paradigm for controlling self-assembly pathways distinct from conventional molecular solvents.
proposals.hypothesis: Ionic liquids, despite their low macroscopic polarity, can direct the formation of specific hydrogen-bonded oligomers and duplexes through their inherent, structured solvent networks, leading to enhanced cooperativity and selectivity in molecular recognition events compared to traditional organic solvents.
proposals.method: We will combine computational virtual screening with quantitative experimental binding studies. Molecular dynamics simulations using OPLS-AA/GAFF force fields for solutes and ILs (e.g., [C4mim][BF4], [C4mim][PF6]) will analyze solute-solvent structuring and pre-organization. Cooperativity in H-bonded duplex formation for known oligomers (e.g., acylated aminopyridines) will be measured in ILs versus conventional solvents using NMR and UV/vis titrations, applying Hunter's established methodologies for quantifying interaction energies and chelate cooperativity. The experimental data will be used to validate and refine the computational models of solvent-solute interactions.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative framework for predicting and exploiting ionic liquid solvent effects in supramolecular chemistry and crystal engineering. It will provide new design rules for selecting solvents that actively template desired interactions, potentially leading to more efficient discovery of novel cocrystals with improved properties, as demonstrated in our prior virtual screening work.
proposals.novelty: This directly challenges the conventional solvent polarity paradigm by investigating how low-polarity but highly structured ionic liquid solvents can actively direct bond formation, a concept not systematically explored in pre-2026 supramolecular assembly or cocrystal screening studies.

proposals.title: Solvent-Directed Selection in Ternary Supramolecular Systems: Quantifying Cooperativity and Pathway Complexity
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular synthesis for functional materials and pharmaceutical cocrystals often relies on solvent choice to direct assembly, but this is typically studied for binary systems. In more complex, multi-component mixtures—common in real-world formulations—the predictive rules for solvent control are poorly understood. Understanding how solvent modulates the competitive formation of different oligomers or co-assemblies from a pool of complementary building blocks is crucial for achieving high-fidelity self-assembly and designing complex molecular networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in ternary systems containing three complementary hydrogen-bonding components, solvent polarity and hydrogen-bonding capacity will not merely bias equilibrium but will selectively activate specific cooperative interaction networks, leading to distinct, solvent-specific supramolecular products that are not simply linear combinations of binary outcomes.
proposals.method: We will employ a combined computational and experimental strategy. First, virtual cocrystal screening (VCS) protocols, as used for adefovir, will be adapted using Crystal Structure Prediction (CSP) methods with force fields like FIT or anisotropic atom-atom potentials to model ternary systems in different solvent environments. Experimentally, we will synthesize a model system of three complementary ditopic monomers (e.g., diamidopyridine, naphthalenediimide, and carboxylic acid derivatives) and use solvent-vapor diffusion for crystallization. The products will be characterized via X-ray diffraction, solution-phase NMR titrations, and isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) to quantify interaction strengths and cooperativity, directly linking to our prior quantitative frameworks.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a foundational methodology for predicting and controlling assembly pathways in multi-component mixtures, moving beyond binary thinking. It will provide quantitative, solvent-specific cooperativity parameters for ternary networks and deliver new design principles for generating complex supramolecular architectures with potential applications in multi-drug cocrystals and organic electronic materials.
proposals.novelty: While solvent effects on dimerization are known, systematically probing how solvent acts as a selector between three or more competing components to yield unique cooperative assemblies is unexplored. The integration of virtual screening for ternary systems with quantitative experimental validation of network cooperativity represents a significant methodological advance.

proposals.title: Solvent-Directed Assembly of Supramolecular Gels via Switchable H-Bonded Cross-Linkers
proposals.motivation: The rational design of stimuli-responsive soft materials requires precise control over intermolecular interactions to dictate network formation and material properties. While solvent polarity is known to modulate H-bond strength, its systematic exploitation to program the assembly, cross-link density, and rheology of supramolecular gels remains underexplored. This approach offers a powerful, non-covalent route to tunable materials for controlled release or sensing, building directly on pre-2026 advances in quantifying H-bond cooperativity in networks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that oligomeric cross-linkers featuring designed intramolecular H-bonding motifs will exhibit solvent-switchable dimerization, enabling the reversible and predictable formation of polymer gels where solvent polarity directly controls the elastic modulus and mesh size of the network.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of bis-ureidopyrimidinone (UPy) derivatives with tunable intramolecular H-bonding, informed by our prior computational/experimental cocrystal screening workflows. Their dimerization constants in solvents of varying polarity (e.g., chloroform to DMSO) will be quantified using NMR and UV-Vis titrations, correlating with DFT-calculated interaction energies. These cross-linkers will be copolymerized with poly(ethylene glycol) diacrylates via free-radical polymerization. Gel rheology and swelling will be characterized and correlated with solvent history and cross-linker binding constants.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a quantitative, predictive framework for designing solvent-responsive gels by linking molecular-scale interaction parameters (cooperativity, solvent affinity) to bulk material properties. It will deliver new, dynamically tunable gel systems with applications in adaptive materials and provide a generalizable strategy for solvent-directed synthesis beyond crystallization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct translation of quantitative, solvent-dependent H-bonding parameters—previously measured in small-molecule networks—into the programmable assembly of polymeric materials, moving beyond serendipitous discovery to a design-led approach for soft matter.

proposals.title: Inverse Solvent Design for Supramolecular Assembly: A Computational Framework to Engineer Solvent Mixtures for Targeted Hydrogen-Bonded Networks
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular synthesis and crystal engineering, particularly for pharmaceuticals, are critically dependent on solvent choice, which governs solubility, nucleation, and the final intermolecular bonding network. Current solvent selection is largely empirical or relies on trial-and-error screening, creating a bottleneck in the reliable fabrication of designed organic solids. An inverse design methodology, where the solvent is computationally tailored to stabilize a specific target structure, would represent a paradigm shift in materials and pharmaceutical development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by combining explicit-solvent molecular dynamics (MD) simulations with machine learning (ML) surrogate models, we can inversely design optimal solvent compositions that maximize the thermodynamic stability of a pre-defined hydrogen-bonded supramolecular motif or cocrystal synthon.
proposals.method: The method involves three stages. First, we will use high-throughput explicit-solvent MD (using GROMACS/OpenMM) to simulate the stability of a target dimer or oligomer (e.g., a bosentan or adefovir synthon) across a vast combinatorial space of solvent mixtures. Second, we will train a graph neural network (GNN) or a gradient-boosted tree model (XGBoost) on this simulation data to learn the complex function mapping solvent composition and properties to synthon stability metrics. Finally, we will employ a Bayesian optimization loop to iteratively query this surrogate model and propose new solvent mixtures that maximize a stability objective function, effectively solving the inverse design problem.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational pipeline for the rational design of solvent environments, moving beyond qualitative rules. It will provide fundamental insights into how solvent mixtures cooperatively modulate specific non-covalent interactions. The resulting framework will be a powerful tool for accelerating the discovery of new cocrystals and supramolecular assemblies with desired properties.
proposals.novelty: While computational solvent screening exists, the explicit framing as an *inverse problem*—designing the solvent for a fixed target structure—is novel. The integration of large-scale MD datasets with ML for direct optimization of solvent composition, rather than mere prediction or classification, represents a significant methodological advance over pre-2026 high-throughput virtual screening approaches.

proposals.title: Inverted Host-Guest Architectures: Large Aromatic Oligomers as Hosts for Metalloporphyrin π-Stacking Guests
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular chemistry has extensively studied metalloporphyrins as hosts for planar aromatic guests via π-stacking. However, the inverse architecture—using a large, pre-organized aromatic oligomer as the host to bind a metalloporphyrin guest—remains largely unexplored. This inversion challenges conventional design rules and could reveal new principles for controlling binding affinity and cooperativity in multi-component assemblies, with implications for designing functional materials and understanding biomolecular recognition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that large, shape-persistent aromatic oligomers, such as oligo(phenylene ethynylene) or expanded porphyrin derivatives, can act as effective hosts for metalloporphyrins through multipoint π-stacking interactions, and that the binding strength and cooperativity will be highly sensitive to the oligomer's length, curvature, and electronic properties.
proposals.method: The project will combine computational virtual screening and experimental validation. First, we will use molecular dynamics (MD) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations to model the interaction geometries and binding energies between a library of designed aromatic oligomers and metalloporphyrins (e.g., Zn(II)TPP). Promising host candidates will then be synthesized. Binding will be quantified in solution using isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and UV-Vis/fluorescence titrations to measure association constants and thermodynamic parameters, while cooperativity will be assessed via Hunter's established methods for analyzing H-bonded networks.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish design principles for inverted host-guest systems based on π-stacking, expanding the toolbox of supramolecular synthons. It will provide quantitative, fundamental insights into how binding affinity scales with host size and pre-organization. The findings could lead to new cocrystal or co-assembly strategies for pharmaceutical and materials science, where controlling the spatial arrangement of functional porphyrins is critical.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in systematically inverting the conventional roles in porphyrin-based π-stacking, using the metalloporphyrin as the guest rather than the host. This approach moves beyond simple dimeric stacking to explore how extended, tunable aromatic surfaces can act as selective hosts, a concept not widely investigated in pre-2026 supramolecular chemistry.

proposals.title: Design and Validation of Ternary π-Stacked Complexes via Cooperative Hydrogen-Bond Networks
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular assembly often relies on metal coordination for structural integrity and directionality, which can introduce toxicity, cost, and synthetic complexity, limiting applications in pharmaceuticals and materials. The pre-2026 challenge is to achieve similarly robust, multi-component architectures using only non-covalent organic interactions. This work directly addresses the need for biocompatible, tunable assemblies with predictable stoichiometry and stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by designing complementary hydrogen-bonding (H-bond) arrays with pre-organized geometries, it is possible to drive the formation of stable, discrete ternary π-stacked complexes without metal ions, where cooperativity within the H-bond network compensates for the lack of coordination bonds.
proposals.method: The project will combine computational virtual screening and experimental validation, leveraging Dr. Hunter's established expertise. First, we will use molecular dynamics (MD) and density functional theory (DFT) calculations to design and screen candidate trimers featuring a central electron-deficient π-system (e.g., a naphthalenediimide derivative) flanked by two electron-rich partners, linked by designed oligoamide strands with programmed H-bond donor/acceptor sites. Promising candidates will be synthesized, and complex formation in solution will be quantified using NMR titrations and UV-Vis spectroscopy to measure binding constants and cooperativity factors, analogous to prior work on H-bond networks. Solid-state confirmation will be sought via cocrystallization trials.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a new design principle for creating multi-component supramolecular stacks, expanding the toolbox of metal-free functional materials. It will provide quantitative data on how cooperativity in purely organic systems can control assembly stoichiometry, informing the design of next-generation organic electronics and drug co-crystals with improved properties.
proposals.novelty: While π-stacking and H-bonding are well-studied individually, their integrated, cooperative use to enforce a specific ternary architecture—mimicking the precision of metallo-supramolecular chemistry but without metals—is a significant and non-obvious advance beyond binary complex formation.

proposals.title: Predictive Multivariate Models for Ternary Complex Stability from Quantified Ligand Steric and Electronic Descriptors
proposals.motivation: The rational design of supramolecular assemblies, such as drug cocrystals or functional materials, relies on controlling non-covalent interactions. While cooperativity in H-bonded networks is understood, predictive models linking quantified ligand properties to the stability of higher-order ternary complexes, which involve intricate intramolecular and intermolecular balances, are lacking. This gap hinders the efficient design of assemblies with targeted stability and function.
proposals.hypothesis: The stability constant (log K) of a ternary complex, formed via axial ligand coordination to a pre-organized duplex or oligomer, can be quantitatively predicted from a multivariate model based on the ligand's steric and electronic descriptors.
proposals.method: A diverse library of axial pyridine derivatives will be synthesized, with steric (e.g., Charton/Taft parameters, %VBur) and electronic (e.g., Hammett σp, computed electrostatic potential minima) descriptors quantified experimentally and via DFT (B3LYP/6-31G*). Ternary complex stability with a model metalloporphyrin dimer or H-bonded duplex will be measured by ITC and UV-Vis titration. Multivariate analysis (e.g., PLS regression) will correlate descriptors (X-matrix) with measured log K values (Y-matrix) to generate a predictive quantitative structure-stability relationship (QSSR) model.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first general, predictive model for ternary complex assembly based on quantifiable ligand properties, moving the field from qualitative observation to rational design. It will provide a validated computational screening protocol for identifying optimal ligands in supramolecular chemistry and pharmaceutical solid form selection, directly extending the lab's expertise in cooperativity and virtual cocrystal screening.
proposals.novelty: The approach moves beyond analyzing single interactions or binary complexes to model the integrated outcome of multiple competing interactions (metal-ligand, π-π, solvation) in a ternary assembly. It applies rigorous physical organic descriptor quantification and multivariate analysis, common in drug discovery, to the fundamental problem of supramolecular complex stability.

proposals.title: Mapping Solution-Phase Architecture of Ternary π-Stacked Complexes through Integrated FRET and Paramagnetic Relaxation Enhancement NMR
proposals.motivation: Understanding the structure of multicomponent π-stacked complexes, such as drug-coformer assemblies or supramolecular oligomers, is crucial for rational materials and pharmaceutical design. Current structural knowledge is largely limited to static crystal structures, which may not reflect the dynamic, solution-phase architectures governing function and binding cooperativity. A method to determine these structures directly in solution would bridge a critical gap between solid-state characterization and functional behavior.
proposals.hypothesis: The integrated application of site-specific Förster Resonance Energy Transfer (FRET) and Paramagnetic Relaxation Enhancement (PRE) NMR will provide sufficient geometric constraints to build a robust, dynamic structural model of a ternary π-stacked complex in solution, revealing its stoichiometry, relative component orientation, and degree of conformational flexibility.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of complementary π-conjugated oligomers (e.g., modified naphthalenediimides or pyrenes) with site-specific fluorophore (donor/acceptor) and stable nitroxide radical (TEMPO) labels. FRET efficiency measurements between terminal units will yield precise distance distributions for key vectors within the putative sandwich complex. These constraints will serve as inputs for simulated annealing and molecular dynamics (MD) structural refinement using the XPLOR-NIH package. The model will be validated and further refined by comparing experimental PRE-derived intensity attenuations in 1H NMR spectra with those back-calculated from candidate structures.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated spectroscopic protocol for the solution-phase structural analysis of non-covalent ternary assemblies, moving beyond the crystallization bottleneck. It will provide fundamental insights into the interplay of cooperativity and flexibility in π-stacked networks, directly informing the design of functional supramolecular systems and pharmaceutical cocrystals with optimized solution-phase properties.
proposals.novelty: While FRET and PRE-NMR are established techniques, their fully integrated, constraint-driven application to elucidate the complete architecture of dynamic, ternary π-stacked complexes in solution is unprecedented. This approach directly addresses the cooperativity and intramolecular folding themes in Hunter's work, but for ill-defined, multi-component solution aggregates.

proposals.title: Mapping the Conformational Landscape of π-Stacked Ternary Complexes: Correlating Flexible Axial Linker Geometry with Supramolecular Structure
proposals.motivation: Supramolecular architectures based on π-stacking are central to molecular electronics and crystal engineering, yet design principles often rely on rigid, pre-organized components. The role of flexible, multi-dentate linkers in mediating and modulating π-π interactions within ternary complexes remains poorly understood. A systematic crystallographic study is needed to move beyond static models and quantify how conformational freedom in the linker directly influences the geometry and electronic coupling of the stacked aromatic surfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: The conformational flexibility of axial linkers in ternary complexes is not a source of disorder but a design feature that enables a predictable range of π-stacking geometries (distance, offset, overlap), where specific linker conformations correlate with optimized, co-facial alignment of the aromatic platforms.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a series of ternary complexes using a consistent aromatic platform (e.g., a porphyrin or large polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon) and systematically vary the flexibility and denticity of the axial linker (e.g., alkyl chains of different lengths, oligoethylene glycols, simple multi-dentate amines). High-resolution X-ray crystallographic data will be collected for each complex. Structural parameters (π-stacking distance, centroid offset, dihedral angles, linker torsion angles) will be extracted and analyzed using Cambridge Structural Database (CSD) software (Mercury, ConQuest). Statistical analysis and molecular mechanics (MMFF) conformational searches will correlate linker conformation with stacking outcomes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first quantitative, structure-based map linking axial linker flexibility to π-stacking geometry in discrete assemblies. It will establish concrete design rules for using flexible tethers to achieve desired intermolecular interactions, directly impacting the rational design of functional materials and cocrystals. The dataset will serve as a benchmark for computational models of non-covalent interactions in dynamic systems.
proposals.novelty: While Hunter's prior work quantifies cooperativity in H-bond networks and uses virtual screening for cocrystals, this proposal uniquely applies a crystallographic 'mapping' strategy to elucidate the active role of flexible linkers—rather than rigid scaffolds—in defining π-stacking parameters, a dynamic aspect neglected in most static structural analyses.

proposals.title: Deconvoluting High-Affinity π-π Stacking Thermodynamics via Isothermal Titration Calorimetry Competitive Displacement Assays
proposals.motivation: Quantifying the thermodynamics of strong π-π stacking interactions is crucial for understanding molecular recognition in supramolecular chemistry, crystal engineering, and drug design. Direct measurement via isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) often fails for high-affinity (Ka > 10^8 M^-1) systems due to stoichiometric binding, limiting insight into the enthalpy-entropy contributions that drive these interactions. A robust method to access this thermodynamic data for tight-binding π-stacking systems is therefore a significant unmet need in physical organic chemistry.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a competitive displacement assay using ITC, with a pre-characterized weak π-stacking reporter complex, can be used to accurately determine the absolute binding enthalpy (ΔH) and entropy (ΔS) of strong, non-covalent π-π stacking interactions that are inaccessible to direct measurement.
proposals.method: We will first establish and thermodynamically characterize (via direct ITC) a model host-guest system with a weak, reversible π-stacking interaction (e.g., a substituted naphthalene diimide host and an electron-rich arene guest). This forms the reporter complex. Subsequently, a tight-binding π-stacking competitor will be titrated into the pre-formed reporter complex. The resulting heat changes from the displacement reaction will be analyzed using a competitive binding model within the ITC data analysis software (e.g., in MicroCal PEAQ-ITC) to deconvolute the ΔH and Ka (and thus ΔG and ΔS) of the strong interaction. The method will be validated using a series of progressively stronger binders with overlapping affinity ranges.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first generalizable ITC-based protocol for obtaining full thermodynamic profiles of high-affinity π-π stacking interactions. It will yield benchmark data to test and refine computational models of stacking energetics and enable rational design in fields reliant on strong aromatic interactions, such as the engineering of functional organic materials and pharmaceutical cocrystals.
proposals.novelty: While competitive displacement ITC is established in biochemistry for studying protein-ligand interactions, its adaptation to deconvolute the thermodynamics of specific, strong non-covalent interactions between small organic molecules—particularly π-π stacking—is novel and non-trivial, requiring careful design of the reporter system to ensure pure displacement behavior.

proposals.title: Quantifying Cooperativity and Selectivity in Multi-Component Competitive Binding to a Porphyrin Dimer Sandwich Complex
proposals.motivation: Pre-2026 supramolecular chemistry has extensively characterized the thermodynamics of 1:1 and 1:2 host-guest complexes, such as the binding of di-topic guests to porphyrin dimers. However, real-world applications in sensing, catalysis, and materials science involve complex mixtures where multiple guests compete for binding sites. The fundamental principles governing selectivity, amplification, and emergent cooperativity in such multi-component, competitive systems remain poorly quantified and understood.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that in a competitive multi-guest system binding to a porphyrin dimer, the observed selectivity and effective binding constants will not be a simple linear combination of binary affinities, but will be governed by non-additive cooperative interactions within the host-guest network, leading to unexpected amplification or suppression of binding for specific guests.
proposals.method: We will synthesize a model zinc-porphyrin dimer host with a well-defined, flexible linker. A library of di-topic nitrogenous ligands (e.g., DABCO, bipyridines, and custom oligomers) with varying lengths and H-bonding capabilities will be used as competing guests. Binding will be studied via isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) and UV-Vis/fluorescence titrations in non-competitive (single guest) and competitive (ternary and quaternary) mixtures. Data will be analyzed using global fitting to a comprehensive thermodynamic model incorporating all possible host-guest stoichiometries. Molecular dynamics simulations (using GROMACS/AMBER) with explicit solvent will probe the structural origins of observed cooperativity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first quantitative, experimentally validated framework for predicting binding outcomes in complex, multi-component supramolecular systems. It will establish design rules for achieving high-fidelity molecular recognition in competitive environments and provide a direct bridge between model studies and functional applications in dynamic combinatorial chemistry.
proposals.novelty: While Hunter's prior work quantified cooperativity in defined H-bonded networks, this project uniquely scales the complexity to dynamic, competitive equilibria involving multiple different guests, moving beyond the study of pre-formed assemblies to the analysis of real-time binding competition—a significant step towards modeling biological and materials-relevant environments.

ideas.title: Crystallographic Mapping of Dynamic π-Stacking in Ternary Complexes with Flexible Axial Linkers
ideas.description: This research direction combines the synthesis of ternary complexes using conformationally flexible or multi-dentate axial ligands with high-resolution X-ray crystallographic analysis. The core objective is to obtain a series of crystal structures that capture the diverse binding modes and geometries adopted by the flexible linkers as they mediate π-stacking between aromatic platforms. By comparing these structures, we can directly correlate linker flexibility with measurable outcomes like π-stacking distance, overlap, and co-facial alignment, moving beyond the static picture provided by rigid systems. This will establish fundamental design principles for achieving optimal electronic communication through π-stacking in dynamically assembled architectures, where the linker is not merely a passive tether but an active participant in defining the supramolecular geometry.

ideas.title: Probing Non-Covalent Coupling in Metal-Free Assemblies via Single-Molecule Double-Mutant Force Spectroscopy
ideas.description: This research direction combines single-molecule force spectroscopy (SMFS) and double-mutant cycle analysis to study the mechanical design principles of metal-free, ternary π-stacked complexes held together solely by hydrogen bonds and van der Waals forces. By systematically introducing structural mutations into the interacting modules and performing force-extension measurements, the project will construct double-mutant cycles to quantify the coupling free energy between specific non-covalent interactions under mechanical load. This approach reveals how these weak forces cooperate to create a mechanically resilient supramolecular architecture in the absence of strong metal coordination, providing fundamental insights for designing robust bio-inspired and organic materials. The synergy lies in using precise mechanical perturbation to dissect interaction networks that are inherently dynamic and difficult to resolve in bulk, moving beyond static structural analysis to a functional understanding of mechanical cooperativity.

ideas.title: Quantitative Structure-Stability Mapping for Ternary Complex Assembly
ideas.description: This study systematically synthesizes and characterizes a diverse library of axial ligands with quantified steric and electronic properties. For each ligand, the stability constants of the resulting ternary complexes are measured using techniques like isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC) or UV-Vis titration. The experimental stability data is then correlated with the computed and experimental ligand descriptors via multivariate analysis to develop a predictive model. This model enables the rational design of optimal ligands for targeted complex stability and specific π-π interaction strengths, moving beyond simple observation to predictive control.

These constraints are then refined and validated using PRE-NMR with spin-labeled components, which reports on distances and relative orientations. The integrated data from these complementary techniques builds a robust, dynamic structural model of the ternary complex under native, non-crystalline conditions.

ideas.title: Apply ITC with competitive displacement assays to deconvolute π-π stacking thermodynamics in complex systems
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from drug discovery—the ITC competitive displacement assay—to solve the problem of measuring the thermodynamics of very strong (high-affinity) π-π stacking interactions, which often fall outside the optimal binding constant range for direct ITC measurement. In the analogous field, researchers use a known weak ligand as a reporter to indirectly determine the thermodynamics of a tight-binding drug candidate. Here, we propose to establish a model host-guest system with a weak, thermodynamically characterized π-stacking reporter interaction. By titrating a strong π-stacking molecule of interest into this pre-formed complex and analyzing the heat changes from the displacement reaction, we can deconvolute and accurately measure the enthalpy (ΔH) and entropy (ΔS) contributions specific to the strong π-π stacking interaction, enabling thermodynamic studies in more complex, high-affinity molecular systems.

ideas.title: In-Cell FRET-Based Double-Mutant Cycle Analysis of Ternary Complex Stability Modulated by Axial Ligands
ideas.description: This research direction applies FRET-based double-mutant cycle analysis within living cells to study ternary complexes, specifically focusing on how the identity of the axial ligand influences complex stability and assembly. By genetically encoding FRET pairs into the protein components of the complex and introducing systematic mutations, the approach will measure the coupling energies between the axial ligand binding site and key residues involved in π-π interactions directly in the cellular milieu. This allows for the deconvolution of the energetic contributions of specific ligand-protein contacts from the overall stacking interactions under physiologically relevant conditions, including cellular crowding and competing metabolites. The result is a dynamic, quantitative map of how different axial ligands reprogram the interaction network and stability of a ternary complex inside a living cell.

ideas.title: High-resolution mapping of interaction fragility in biomolecular assemblies via multi-site disruption cycles
ideas.description: This research direction extends the double-mutant cycle framework to large, multi-subunit complexes (like the ribosome, spliceosome, or viral capsids) but inverts its typical application. Instead of measuring the energetic gain from forming an interaction, the approach uses cycles of multi-site mutations to systematically disrupt a single, specific interaction (e.g., a hydrogen bond or salt bridge) buried within the assembly. By comparing the destabilization effects of the targeted disruption in different structural and mutational contexts across the complex, it quantifies the interaction's 'fragility'—its susceptibility to disruption and its role in the cooperative network. This provides a high-resolution map of which precise interactions are linchpins for stability and which are more dispensable or context-dependent, offering new insights for targeting allosteric sites or engineering complex stability.

ideas.description: This approach reframes the study of non-covalent interactions by applying the cycle framework to map both the disruptive and compensatory effects of mutations. It systematically compares the energetic and structural consequences of mutations that break a specific interaction against a background of global stability measurements and conformational analysis. This dual measurement reveals whether the functional or stability loss is absolute or is offset by the formation of alternative stabilizing contacts. The result is a more robust, unbiased understanding of interaction networks, distinguishing between truly essential 'linchpin' contacts and those that are part of a redundant or evolvable system.

ideas.title: Use NMR Relaxation Dispersion for Dynamic Allosteric Coupling in Double-Mutant Cycles
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from macromolecular crystallography, where correlated atomic motions are inferred from temperature-dependent changes in B-factors. The analogous method is applied to NMR spectroscopy to measure dynamic coupling in double-mutant cycles. Specifically, CPMG relaxation dispersion experiments are performed at multiple magnetic field strengths on wild-type and single/double mutant proteins. By analyzing how the millisecond-timescale dynamics (quantified by exchange parameters Rex and kex) at one position are altered by a mutation at a second, coupled position, one can extract a dynamic coupling energy. This quantifies the allosteric communication between the two sites through their shared influence on a common conformational exchange process, providing a direct NMR-based measure of dynamic interaction coupling that complements traditional thermodynamic coupling analyses.

ideas.description: This research direction combines inverse design with a generalized double-mutant cycle framework that relaxes the requirement for a single wild-type background. Instead, it uses cycle data collected from multiple reference states—such as different conformational states, ligand-bound forms, or environmental conditions—as simultaneous constraints for an inverse problem. This allows the engineering of protein interaction interfaces where the coupling energies between mutations are explicitly designed to be sensitive to the background state. The result is a method to design proteins with interaction profiles that can be switched or tuned by cellular context, enabling the creation of novel allosteric systems or environment-responsive biomaterials.

ideas.title: Genome-wide FRET-enabled Synthetic Genetic Arrays for Direct Mapping of Protein Interaction Dynamics in Genetic Networks
ideas.description: This research direction integrates FRET-based molecular rulers into a yeast Synthetic Genetic Array (SGA) screening platform. By tagging key proteins in a pathway of interest with FRET donor and acceptor pairs (e.g., porphyrin-based and guest fluorophores), one can perform genome-wide double-mutant screens where the primary readout is not just growth fitness, but a quantitative FRET efficiency signal reporting on in vivo protein complex conformation or association. This allows the systematic mapping of how thousands of genetic backgrounds modulate the physical architecture and dynamics of a specific ternary protein complex. Consequently, the approach directly links genetic interaction networks to their biophysical underpinnings, revealing whether suppressive or synergistic interactions arise from stabilizing, destabilizing, or altering the geometry of key molecular machines.

ideas.title: Electrochemically-Gated Porphyrin Dimer Host for Selective Sensing of Larger Polycyclic Aromatic Guests
ideas.description: This project designs a porphyrin dimer sandwich host with integrated redox-active linkers, creating a cavity whose size and electron density can be modulated by applied potential. The system is engineered to selectively bind larger, predefined polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbon (PAH) guests, such as coronene or ovalene derivatives, only at specific oxidation states of the host. Binding is directly transduced into a change in electrochemical current or potential, enabling the quantitative detection of specific PAHs. This approach moves beyond simple exploration to create a tunable, signal-generating host-guest system for larger aromatics with potential applications in environmental sensing and molecular electronics.

ideas.title: In-Cell Thermodynamic Profiling of π-π Interactions via FRET-Coupled Double-Mutant Cycle Analysis
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the principles of double-mutant cycle analysis using FRET inside living cells with the thermodynamic rigor of isothermal titration calorimetry (ITC). The core innovation is to use the in-cell FRET efficiency measurements from a comprehensive double-mutant cycle—performed on a π-π stacking interface—to calculate the free energy of interaction (ΔG). By systematically performing parallel, high-precision ITC measurements on the same purified protein variants under controlled buffer conditions, the enthalpy (ΔH) and entropy (ΔS) components are determined. The synergy emerges by computationally subtracting the in vitro thermodynamic parameters from the in-cell-derived ΔG, thereby isolating and quantifying the specific enthalpic and entropic impacts of the cellular milieu (e.g., crowding, solvation, post-translational modifications) on the π-π stacking interaction itself.

ideas.title: Integrating DEER spectroscopy with double-mutant cycles to quantify nanosecond dynamic coupling in proteins
ideas.description: This approach combines the thermodynamic rigor of double-mutant cycles with the nanosecond timescale sensitivity of Double Electron-Electron Resonance (DEER) spectroscopy. Site-directed spin labeling is used to introduce paramagnetic probes at positions of interest within a protein, and DEER measures the distance distribution and dynamics between these probes. By constructing double-mutant cycles where both the spin-labeled sites and a distal functional site are mutated, the dynamic coupling energy between them is quantified from changes in the DEER-derived distance profiles. This integrated system directly measures the fast, functionally critical motions that govern allosteric communication, overcoming the timescale limitations of conventional NMR.

ideas.title: Evolutionary-Informed Quantum Mechanics for Predicting Allosteric Communication Networks
ideas.description: This research direction integrates statistical coupling analysis (SCA) from bioinformatics with quantum mechanical (QM) interaction energy calculations. First, SCA is applied to protein sequence families to identify pairs of residues predicted to be functionally coupled, suggesting they may participate in key interactions for allostery or stability. Second, for top-ranked pairs, QM calculations (e.g., DFT or accurate wavefunction methods) are performed on relevant molecular fragments to compute the intrinsic interaction energy (π-π, electrostatic, dispersion) in a controlled, environment-free context. This combined approach validates the physical plausibility of evolutionary couplings and provides an energetic map, transforming co-evolutionary signals into a quantifiable, energy-based model of protein communication networks for guiding targeted mutagenesis and interpreting double-mutant cycles.

ideas.title: Adapting Perturbation Theory from Electrical Engineering to Map Energetic Networks in Biomolecular Assemblies
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to extend double-mutant cycle methodology to large, multi-component biomolecular assemblies, where exhaustive pairwise mutagenesis is impractical. Drawing an analogy from Electrical Engineering, where perturbation theory is used to map complex circuits by applying sparse inputs and measuring propagated outputs, we adapt this solution. The adapted research idea treats the large biomolecular complex as an energetic network. By performing a strategically chosen, sparse set of single and double mutations at presumed key 'node' positions (e.g., at subunit interfaces or conserved structural motifs), and measuring high-throughput functional or biophysical readouts, one can employ a perturbation-theory-inspired computational model. This model would infer the coupling energies between distant sites, effectively reconstructing the interaction network and revealing critical energetic pathways that govern the stability and function of the entire assembly, thus achieving the goal of the double-mutant cycle in a scalable way.

ideas.title: Genome-wide mapping of porphyrin-protein interactions via synthetic genetic array logic
ideas.description: This research fuses quantitative biophysical measurement with genome-wide genetics by applying a synthetic genetic array (SGA) screen to yeast strains engineered with a library of porphyrin-binding proteins. Each strain expresses a variant of a heme-binding protein engineered with different aromatic residues, creating a defined gradient of π-π interaction strengths with the porphyrin cofactor. These strains are then crossed into a genome-wide deletion mutant array. The resulting double-mutant fitness data maps how specific, physically quantified porphyrin-protein interaction energies modify genetic interaction networks, revealing which cellular pathways are most sensitive to subtle changes in cofactor binding physics.

ideas.title: Adapting Network Tomography for Single-Molecule Mechanical Coupling
ideas.description: This idea expands the original double-mutant cycle analysis by drawing an analogy from computer network diagnostics (network tomography). In that field, the internal performance of network links is inferred by sending coordinated probe packets from multiple external points and analyzing the correlated outcomes. Adapted to single-molecule force spectroscopy, the solution involves performing force spectroscopy not just on wild-type and double mutants, but on a designed series of double (and higher-order) mutants, applying force in different, systematically varied directions (e.g., pulling on different structural domains or termini). The correlated changes in the resulting force-extension spectra—such as shifts in unfolding peaks, changes in contour length, or alterations in intermediate states—are then analyzed using inverse methods. This allows for the tomographic reconstruction of the mechanical interaction network within the protein, revealing how force propagates and how mutations mechanically couple beyond the directly perturbed sites, providing a more holistic map of mechanical allostery.

ideas.title: Testing Additivity in Protein Energetics via Statistical Mechanics of Non-Interacting Spins
ideas.description: This idea challenges the core assumption of strictly additive energetic effects for mutations at non-interacting sites by drawing an analogy from statistical mechanics. In condensed matter physics, the Ising model for non-interacting spins predicts perfect additivity of magnetization, an assumption tested by applying perturbing fields to reveal hidden couplings. We adapt this solution by treating mutations at distant sites as 'non-interacting spins' within a protein's energy landscape. The research would introduce a carefully chosen environmental perturbation (e.g., a ligand binding event or a change in solution conditions) that could act as a 'field,' potentially revealing latent energetic coupling between the mutated sites. By precisely measuring the folding stability (ΔΔG) of single mutants and the double mutant under both native and perturbed conditions, we can test for significant deviations from expected additivity. A systematic departure would indicate that the assumption of non-interaction fails under certain conditions, uncovering context-dependent epistasis and improving models for predicting mutational effects in protein engineering and evolution.

ideas.title: Apply Granger Causality from Econometrics to Predict Residues for Double-Mutant Cycle Experiments
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from econometrics to the problem of selecting residues for double-mutant cycle experiments. In econometrics, Granger causality analysis is used on time series data (like stock prices or economic indicators) to infer predictive relationships and causal directions within complex, interdependent systems. The analogous solution is to apply this framework to molecular dynamics simulation data of a protein. By treating the trajectory of conformational metrics (e.g., side-chain dihedral angles, inter-atomic distances) for each residue as a time series, we can perform a Granger causality test between all residue pairs. Residue pairs where the fluctuations of one residue provide statistically significant predictive power for the fluctuations of another are inferred to have a direct, potentially functionally relevant coupling. This identifies a prioritized, dynamically-informed set of residue pairs for experimental testing via double-mutant cycles, moving beyond static co-evolutionary signals to leverage time-dependent causal inference.

ideas.description: This approach combines a novel FRET biosensor design with a complementary calibration method to achieve reliable double-mutant cycle analysis inside living cells. The biosensor incorporates internal reference fluorophores to continuously correct for cellular environmental variables like viscosity and non-specific binding. In parallel, each mutant construct is measured in a synthetic cytosol medium formulated from cell extracts, providing a calibrated baseline for the interaction energy. This dual strategy isolates the true thermodynamic coupling energy between mutations from the confounding biophysical noise of the intracellular milieu.

ideas.title: A Closed-Loop, Model-Guided Platform for High-Confidence Double-Mutant Cycle Analysis
ideas.description: This system integrates automated molecular biology, high-throughput biophysics, and computational modeling into a single, adaptive platform. It uses initial structural and sequence data to seed a predictive model of pairwise epistasis, which then actively selects the most information-rich double mutants for experimental construction and characterization. Each cycle of measurement is statistically validated in real-time, with the platform automatically repeating experiments or adjusting conditions to ensure robust coupling energy estimates. The resulting high-confidence dataset and refined model directly yield testable mechanistic hypotheses about protein energetics and allosteric networks.

ideas.title: Pathway-validated double-mutant cycle analysis via trajectory-selected single-molecule force spectroscopy
ideas.description: This method performs single-molecule force spectroscopy under force-clamp conditions to collect thousands of unfolding events for wild-type and mutant protein variants. A machine learning classifier is trained on high-precision unfolding trajectories to identify and select only events that conform to a single, dominant unfolding pathway, filtering out heterogeneous or misfolded pulls. Double-mutant cycle analysis is then executed using the mean unfolding forces or kinetics solely from this curated, pathway-homogeneous dataset. This yields a mechanically derived coupling energy that is rigorously decoupled from confounding pathway variability, providing a robust measure of direct inter-residue interaction within the native fold.

ideas.title: Multi-State Reference Framework with Unified Fitness Landscape Mapping
ideas.description: This approach systematically explores evolutionary cycles from varied conformational reference states by embedding them within a unified thermodynamic and kinetic network. A primary reference state is defined, and all other conformational states are characterized by their free energy relative to this state and their transition probabilities. During directed evolution or deep mutational scanning, mutational effects measured from any conformational starting point are transformed into a consistent fitness metric relative to the primary reference. This creates an integrated, multi-perspective fitness landscape that captures state-dependent mutational effects while enabling direct comparison and robust prediction of evolutionary trajectories across different protein functional states.

ideas.title: Adopt NMR-style relaxation dispersion for in-cell FRET kinetics
ideas.description: The original idea proposes using FRET for double-mutant cycle analysis inside living cells. By drawing an analogy from NMR spectroscopy, specifically CPMG relaxation dispersion experiments used to uncover transient protein states, we adapt this solution to the FRET context. The adapted research idea is to adopt NMR-style relaxation dispersion for in-cell FRET kinetics. This involves systematically modulating the temporal sampling parameters of a FRET measurement (like laser pulse sequences) within the cell and analyzing the resulting dispersion in FRET signals. This approach would allow the method to go beyond equilibrium snapshots and quantify the kinetics and populations of short-lived, functionally relevant states of protein complexes or interactions directly in the native cellular milieu, adding a dynamic dimension to in-cell double-mutant cycle analysis.

ideas.title: Adapt Microfluidic Droplet PCR Platform for High-Throughput Double-Mutant Cycle Analysis
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from genomics—microfluidic droplet platforms for massively parallel single-cell analysis—to the problem of high-throughput double-mutant cycle generation. In genomics, platforms like Drop-seq use droplets to isolate individual cells with barcoded beads for parallel RNA sequencing. The adapted solution involves creating an automated platform where microfluidic devices generate droplets, each containing a single protein variant (wild-type, single mutant, or double mutant) along with reagents for a functional or binding assay. Fluorescence readouts within the droplets report on the variant's activity, and fluorescence-activated droplet sorting enables rapid analysis and collection of data. This compartmentalization allows for the simultaneous, high-throughput measurement of interaction energies for all necessary single and double mutants in a cycle, dramatically accelerating the experimental phase of double-mutant cycle analysis.

ideas.title: High-throughput network mapping of cooperativity via constrained mutagenesis and global model fitting
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the combinatorial intractability of exhaustive double-mutant cycles in large networks by combining targeted, high-throughput mutagenesis with computational inference. Instead of constructing all pairwise cycles, it uses prior information (e.g., from evolutionary couplings or coarse-grained simulations) to define a sparse set of key mutational perturbations expected to be most informative for network cooperativity. The phenotypic effects of this library of single and selected double mutants are measured en masse via a deep mutational scan. A global thermodynamic model, parameterizing all pairwise and possibly higher-order interactions within the network, is then fit to the complete high-throughput dataset, simultaneously extracting all cooperative free energies. This yields a quantitative map of cooperativity across the network from a tractable experimental effort.

ideas.title: Decoupling perturbation from interaction via a triple-mutant reference frame
ideas.description: This method introduces a third, carefully validated reference mutation at a structurally inert site to establish a common energetic baseline. By constructing triple mutants containing this reference plus each single mutation of interest, the system measures the true background-adjusted perturbation effect of each mutation. Comparing these refined perturbation magnitudes to the observed double-mutant cycle signal allows for the explicit calculation of the residual, genuine interaction energy, cleanly separating the two phenomena the original approach conflated.

ideas.title: Quantifying the prevalence and mechanisms of non-additivity in protein mutational effects
ideas.description: This project conducts a comprehensive experimental test of mutational additivity by performing a deep mutational scan to measure the fitness or stability effects of all single and double mutations within a model protein domain. The data is analyzed using a statistical mechanical model to separate the total observed epistasis into two components: that which is explainable by a shared dependence on global stability (additive background effect) and the residual, specific epistasis indicative of direct physical interactions or nonlinear local perturbations. This approach directly addresses the confound of global stability, providing a robust, quantitative measure of how often and to what degree mutations deviate from strict additivity, even in the absence of obvious direct contact.

ideas.title: Network Robustness: Quantifying How Mutations Disrupt Interaction Cycles
ideas.description: This idea applies the cycle framework to study how mutations disrupt, rather than create, a specific non-covalent interaction, flipping the perspective from gain to loss. The analogy is drawn from Network Science and Critical Infrastructure Analysis, where system robustness is studied by analyzing failure modes—systematically removing components and measuring the resulting loss of connectivity. The adapted solution treats the protein's interaction network as a graph, with mutations as targeted 'edge removals'. The research would computationally and experimentally map how specific mutations disrupt the minimal cycles (closed interaction paths) essential for a structure or function, quantifying the 'interaction loss' and identifying which cycles are most vulnerable to disruption, analogous to finding critical pathways in a network whose failure causes systemic collapse.

ideas.title: Multi-Reference State Analysis for Functional Landscapes
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that a single, static 'wild-type' sequence or structure is the necessary reference point for all comparative analyses in fields like protein engineering or evolutionary biology. Drawing an analogy from geodesy—where multiple reference ellipsoids (like WGS84 and ITRF) coexist with well-defined transformation models to relate positions between them—the solution is to establish a multi-reference state framework. In this adapted approach, different conformational states, functional states, or environmentally adapted variants are each defined as valid reference 'frames.' The research would develop methods to quantify mutational effects (e.g., on stability, activity, or fitness) not as a single value from one reference, but as a matrix or network of values transformable between references. This allows a more nuanced understanding of how mutations behave across a protein's or system's natural functional landscape, capturing context-dependent effects that are lost when anchored to a single arbitrary 'wild-type.'

ideas.title: A Bayesian Calibration Loop for Mutant Cycle Free Energies
ideas.description: This idea adapts the data assimilation approach from climate science to the problem of integrating mutant cycles with free energy calculations. In climate modeling, disparate observational sources are fused within a Bayesian framework to calibrate and validate complex simulators. Here, experimental double-mutant cycle data (providing precise, context-dependent coupling energies) and alchemical free energy calculations (providing detailed, residue-wise decompositions) are treated as complementary data streams. A computational pipeline is developed where a Bayesian inference engine iteratively adjusts force field parameters, solvation model choices, or systematic error estimates in the free energy calculations to maximize the probabilistic agreement with the ensemble of experimental cycles. This creates a closed-loop calibration system: the experiments validate and ground the computations, while the computationally refined model provides atomistic interpretation and can predict cycles for novel mutants, with rigorously quantified uncertainties for both.

ideas.title: Adapting DNA microarray pooling strategies for high-throughput double-mutant cycle mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts combinatorial pooling strategies from DNA microarray design in genomics to dramatically scale up double-mutant cycle mapping. In early microarray genotyping, researchers used overlapping pool designs (like Shifted Transversal Design) to test thousands of genetic variants against many probes simultaneously, deconvolving individual interactions from aggregate signals. For deep mutational scanning of protein interactions, we would create systematically designed overlapping pools of single mutants, where each variant appears in multiple pools with different partner mutants. After phenotypic selection and next-generation sequencing of pool fitness scores, computational deconvolution algorithms would extract the individual epistatic interaction terms for all double-mutant combinations. This approach could increase throughput by 10-100x compared to testing each double mutant individually, enabling comprehensive interaction maps for larger protein domains or multi-protein complexes.

ideas.title: Context-Aware Genetic Interaction Mapping via Environmentally Multiplexed Synthetic Genetic Arrays
ideas.description: This approach scales yeast synthetic genetic array (SGA) technology to systematically map how genetic interaction networks depend on environmental context. By implementing double-mutant construction and phenotyping across a multiplexed array of distinct culturing conditions in parallel, it generates a three-dimensional dataset of genetic interactions (Gene A, Gene B, Environment). This conditional interaction tensor identifies which synthetic lethal or epistatic relationships are robust across environments versus those that are highly condition-specific, dramatically increasing the physiological relevance and predictive power of the network model. The resulting map provides a foundational resource for understanding genetic buffering, pathway plasticity, and the environmental dependency of genotype-to-phenotype relationships.
